This is not a reason to declare the sprint dead. It is a reason to notice the sprint has been measuring the wrong thing for a while now, and the agent just made the mismatch impossible to ignore.

Ceremonies that keep their form and lose their purpose

Some ceremonies survive the agent's arrival with their real purpose intact, because their purpose was never about the clock. Some do not, and the difference is worth being precise about, because it determines what a delivery team should actually do next.

Backlog grooming produces a tidy backlog on paper. Its real value was the argument, the moment two people with different context disagreed out loud about what mattered and why, and a newer team member learned more from that disagreement than from any onboarding document. An agent can groom a backlog cleanly, checking every item against a definition of ready and routing what is missing an estimate for sizing. What it cannot produce is the disagreement, because there is nothing to disagree with. The backlog comes back tidy and the argument that used to teach people how to think about scope never happens.

The retrospective produces a list of action items. Its real value was the one sanctioned hour where a team could say what was actually broken without it counting as going over someone's head. An agent can summarize the sticky notes and generate a cleaner list than most retros produce today. What it cannot produce is the psychological safety of the room, because the room is not there anymore.

The standup produces a status update. Its real value was the accidental collision, the unplanned exchange where one person's passing comment saved another from a mistake nobody had written down anywhere. A status digest generated from commits and tickets is more accurate than a human reading their yesterday out loud. It is also read by no one in the presence of anyone else, so the collision that used to happen by accident stops happening at all.

This is the pattern to watch for on any delivery team right now: a ceremony that still runs on the calendar, still produces its stated output, and produces it faster and cleaner than before, while the reason the ceremony existed in the first place goes quietly missing. The meeting did not disappear. It became empty ritual, form without the collision it was built to force.

The honest answer: keep the collision, automate the status

Once you see the split between stated function and real function, the path forward stops being a referendum on Scrum and becomes a much narrower decision, ceremony by ceremony.

Anything whose value was always the output, status collection, burndown tracking, ticket hygiene, backlog tidiness, should go to the agent without hesitation. A person reading their own commit history out loud was always a worse version of what a tool can now do automatically.

Anything whose value was the friction, the argument, the collision, the sanctioned hour to say what is wrong, has to be protected on purpose, because nothing about automating a ceremony announces what is being lost. The output looks the same or better. The team feels more efficient. Six months later, nobody can explain why small dependencies keep slipping through, because the mechanism that used to catch them was never on anyone's list of things to preserve.

The practical move for a delivery lead is to stop asking whether the agent can run a given ceremony and start asking whether the ceremony's value was ever the thing it produced, or the thing that happened around producing it. If the former, automate freely and reclaim the time. If the latter, keep the human collision deliberately, even if it now looks inefficient next to a machine that could do the stated task faster. The inefficiency is the point. It is where judgment happens that nothing downstream can recover if it is skipped.

What the delivery role becomes

This is where the role that used to run the ceremonies changes shape, rather than disappearing.

When the mechanics of coordination, status collection, metrics, backlog hygiene, sprint-planning prep, get handed to an agent, the delivery lead is not left with less to do. They are left with the part that was always harder to see: deciding which collisions the team still needs and making sure those collisions happen on purpose, since they will no longer happen by accident inside a ceremony built for a different reason. That means noticing when a team has gone quiet in an unhealthy way, deliberately engineering the moment where two people reconcile conflicting assumptions before either builds on top of them, and watching for a newer failure: an agent running the planning and grooming process itself, making prioritization calls that look neutral and are not, weighting whatever the historical data weighted, with nobody assigned to check its judgment.

That matters more than it sounds like it should. A framework that automates its own coordination layer has quietly created a system that acts on the team's behalf, which needs the same kind of watching this series keeps describing for every other agentic system: someone accountable for what it decided and why, not just for whether the backlog looks tidy afterward. Handing the ceremony to the agent does not remove the need for that person. It removes the ceremony that used to make their job visible.

None of this means Scrum is finished, and it would be too easy to say so. Teams still need to align, still need to decide what happens next, still need the argument that teaches a newer engineer what actually matters and why. The need the ceremonies were built to serve has not gone anywhere. What has gone is the reliable link between running the ceremony and meeting the need, because the agent can now run the ceremony perfectly and meet none of it. The framework is not obsolete. It is no longer sufficient on its own terms, and a team that keeps the calendar while dropping the vigilance will look, for months, exactly like a team that is coordinating well. The standups will happen. The sprints will close. The retro notes will be tidier than they have ever been. The first sign that anything was lost will not show up in the ceremony at all. It will show up later, in the dependency nobody caught, in the assumption nobody argued about out loud, in the moment a newer hire never got to watch two seniors disagree, long after the meeting that used to prevent it stopped needing anyone in the room.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the ceremonies a delivery team relies on lose their original purpose when an agent runs the process at its own pace. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - Is the PM Still the Hero?
entry.content:
Is the PM Still the Hero?

Post 6, The Agentic AI Team series

Search for "product manager" and "agentic AI" together and you will find no shortage of PMs telling other PMs that the agentic era finally makes the PM the center of everything. The engineer writes less code because the agent writes it. The designer's screens matter less because half the product now runs without one. The PM, meanwhile, is the one who still decides what should exist, so the PM becomes the hero of the story.

I have written versions of this claim myself, across two earlier books in this series. It is not wrong, exactly. It is worse than wrong. It is a closed loop, and closed loops are more dangerous than errors, because nothing inside them ever corrects.

A conversation with only one side in the room

Here is the loop. PMs write articles about why the PM is now the most important seat on the team. Other PMs read the articles, agree, and write their own. The articles get shared in PM communities, cited in PM newsletters, quoted at PM conferences. Everyone nodding is a PM.

The engineers and designers who would actually have to agree to this claim, who would have to accept a world where the PM's judgment gates their work more than it used to, are not in the room and are not reading the newsletter. The claim that the PM is the new center of the team is being adjudicated entirely among people who benefit from it being true, and the verdict keeps coming back yes.

This matters for a reason that has nothing to do with vanity. The agentic product needs a specific thing to happen across roles: the architect has to agree to enforce a boundary, the eval owner has to agree a confidence signal is trustworthy, the domain expert has to agree a judgment call sits where the spec says it sits. None of that happens by a PM declaring it in a document that only other PMs read. It happens in a room, or it does not happen at all. A closed loop that never reaches the people whose agreement it needs is rehearsing a speech nobody outside the choir will hear.

Why it is worse than merely incomplete

An incomplete argument is a nuisance. This one is closer to a provocation, landing on ground that was already sensitive. The relationship between product and engineering was never a peaceful division of labor. It has always carried a low hum of who decides and who depends on whom, who gets credit when a feature lands and who gets blamed when it does not. Most teams manage that hum well enough to ship. It does not take much to turn it into a fight.

The PM-as-hero story turns it up, and the spark is not the story alone but what a PM does next while telling it. A PM who has just explained, to an audience of other PMs, why the PM is now the center of the agentic team, and who then vibe-codes a working prototype over a weekend without looping in an engineer, is not demonstrating range. From the engineer's seat, that PM has said two things back to back: I am the one who matters now, and I do not need you to prove it. Neither is a statement an engineer, or a designer, is inclined to sit still for.

The predictable result is withdrawal, not agreement. The more loudly a PM claims the center, the less the rest of the team listens when the PM actually needs them, which in an agentic product is often and early. The boundary the agent operates inside has to be built by an architect who trusts the PM's judgment about where it should sit. The confidence signal that lets an agent defer instead of guess has to be built by an eval owner who believes the PM understands what the signal is for. Claiming the center loudly makes both less willing to build what the PM needs from them. The story is not just talking to the wrong room. It is making the right room harder to walk into afterward.

The honest re-vantage

So let me do something a hero narrative does not usually permit, which is correct my own prior framing in public.

In earlier parts of this series, and in the books it draws from, I described a long list of responsibilities as "the PM's job": where the autonomy boundary sits, which actions get gated behind human approval, how the audit trail gets structured, how the team recovers when the agent gets it wrong. Writing from the PM's seat, all of that looked like PM work, because from that seat, the PM is the one who feels the weight of the missing piece first.

On a real cross-functional team, most of that list does not belong to the PM alone. The boundary is a product decision, but an architect has to enforce it in a way the code cannot route around, a craft the PM does not have and should not pretend to. Which actions get gated behind approval is a shared call between the PM, the domain expert who knows what a wrong action costs, and the designer who has to build an approval moment a tired human will use correctly. The audit trail is specified by whoever needs it during an incident review, and built by the platform team, not authored by the PM in a requirements doc. Treating all of it as PM territory was a category error, mine included, and naming it as one is the only way to move past it.

What is left for the PM, once the over-claimed territory is handed back, is smaller and sharper than the hero story suggests. The PM still owns the go/no-go, the bet that this should be built at all. The PM still owns where the boundary should sit as a business and risk judgment, even though someone else enforces it. The PM still owns which actions matter enough to require a human's eyes before they happen, as one voice in a shared decision, not the only one. And the PM still owns the kind of judgment a tool cannot commoditize: taking responsibility, by name, for a bet made under uncertainty that no amount of agent-generated analysis can make for you. That is a real job. It is just not the whole team's job wearing a PM badge.

The grid, not the crown

The antidote to a role claiming the center is not a different role claiming it instead. It is dropping the question of which role is central and asking, for this specific responsibility, on this specific product, who actually holds it.

Picture a grid instead of a hierarchy. Down one side, the responsibilities an agentic product generates: the go/no-go decision, the boundary placement, the gating of specific actions, the audit surface, the recovery path, the confidence signal an agent uses to decide whether to act or ask. Across the top, the people on the team: PM, engineer, architect, designer, domain expert, eval owner, whoever else the product requires. Every responsibility gets an owner derived from who does the work and who carries the consequence if it is wrong, not from who gave the most convincing conference talk about their own centrality.

The grid does something the hero story cannot. It survives contact with people who were never going to read the hero story in the first place. An architect does not need to believe the PM is the center of the team to accept a cell in a grid that says the architect enforces the boundary the PM proposed. A designer does not need to concede any ground on status to agree the approval screen is theirs to build well. The grid asks for agreement on work. The hero story asks for agreement on rank. Work is the easier one to get, and it is the only one the agentic product actually needs.

A team deciding whether to ship

Consider a feature that lets a support agent resolve refund requests on its own, up to a limit, without a person approving each one. The team includes a PM, an engineer, an architect, a support operations lead, and an eval owner who grades the agent's decisions before launch.

The PM opens with the case for building it: refund requests are a volume problem, the manual queue is the top complaint from support, and a bounded agent could clear the easy cases in seconds instead of hours. That is a real go/no-go, and it belongs to the PM. Nobody else in the room is positioned to weigh that trade-off against everything else competing for the roadmap, or to answer for it if the bet is wrong.

Where should the boundary sit. The PM proposes a starting point, a dollar limit under which the agent can act alone. The support operations lead pushes back with something the PM cannot see from the roadmap: dollar amount is a weak proxy for risk, since a small refund on a flagged account is more dangerous than a large one on a clean account. The architect adds a constraint from the other direction: whatever rule gets chosen has to be enforced at the point the money actually moves, not just checked earlier in the flow, or the boundary is a suggestion instead of a wall. The final boundary is none of their opening positions. It is a joint answer, and the PM's contribution was proposing where to start and owning the consequence of wherever it lands, not dictating the number.

Which refund requests get gated behind a human. The eval owner has data the others do not: the agent's confidence signal is well calibrated for standard refunds and unreliable for anything touching a disputed charge. That single fact reshapes the gate more than anything the PM or the support lead proposed. The PM still has a vote, since the cost of an unnecessary escalation is a business cost, but the PM does not have standing to override a calibration finding with a hunch.

Every responsibility found an owner. None of them needed to believe the PM was the center of the team, or that the architect or the eval owner was. Each needed to believe the responsibility handed to them matched what they actually knew how to judge. That agreement held because it never asked anyone to concede status. It only asked them to do the part of the work that was theirs.

What the loop was protecting

Go back to the closed loop of PMs writing for PMs about PM centrality, and ask what it was actually protecting. Not the team. A loop that never reaches the people it needs to persuade cannot be protecting a working relationship with them, because it never tested one. What it protects is a feeling, the feeling of being the one who matters, rehearsed where nobody can say otherwise.

The grid does not offer that feeling. It offers something a hero story cannot: an architect who enforces a boundary because the boundary is sound, not because the PM is owed deference, and an eval owner whose calibration finding changes a gate because the finding is correct, not because it flatters anyone's seat at the table. That is a smaller win than being the hero. It is also the only kind that survives the moment an engineer, a designer, or an architect actually reads what was written about them, and decides whether to agree.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the PM's real authority narrows and sharpens once the responsibilities claimed on the team's behalf are handed back to the people who actually hold them. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Empty Column
entry.content:
The Empty Column

Post 7, The Agentic AI Team series

Take any team that ships an agentic product and ask them to draw their team structure on a whiteboard. They will draw it fast, because they have drawn it a hundred times before. A product manager decides what to build. An architect decides how it fits together. Engineers build it. Operations runs it once it ships. Four boxes, one arrow running left to right, and everyone in the room nods, because that is what a software team has looked like for twenty years.

The drawing is not wrong. That is the uncomfortable part. It is just incomplete in a way nobody in the room can see, because the model they drew was built for a different kind of product than the one they are now shipping, and the model does not know it is missing anything.

The old model is not the problem

It is worth saying plainly: the product manager, architect, engineer, operations lineup is a good model. It did not become good by accident. It maps cleanly onto the work of building something, deciding intent, shaping structure, constructing it, running it, and every team that has ever shipped software has run some version of that sequence. Two decades of reorganizations added seats to it, never replaced it. That durability is earned.

So this is not an argument that the model is broken or that the roles in it are obsolete. It is an argument that the model was built to staff one pipeline, and an agentic product needs two.

A grid, not a row

Picture the same four stages, intent, structure, build, operate, as a row across the top of a grid. That row is what the old model staffs, and it staffs it well. Now add a second row beneath it, because an agentic product is not one thing to build. It is two.

The first row builds the agent: what it should do, how it should be shaped, its components, running it as a live service. The second row supervises the agent: what has to be true before anyone trusts it, how the supervision itself is structured and enforced, the guardrails and instruments and recovery paths, and the ongoing work of watching it once it is live and stopping it when it drifts.

Call the first row Channel 1 and the second Channel 2. Draw them together and you get a grid: two rows, four columns, eight cells. Every cell is a piece of work that either has an owner on your team or does not.

Here is what that grid looks like, in the plainest form:

  Intent Structure Build Operate

Channel 1 (build it)what it's forhow it's shapedthe componentsrun it as a service
Channel 2 (supervise it)what must be true to trust itwhat gets enforced vs. requestedguardrails, instruments, recoverywatch it, stop it, recover it

Fill in the top row on almost any team and it fills in fast, because that row is the work everyone already knows how to divide. Fill in the bottom row, and watch what happens.

The column that comes up empty

Run this exercise on a real team, without flattering the results, and the pattern is not subtle. Ask who owns deciding what must be true before this agent is trusted, and the answer is usually the product manager, halfway, because nobody else has claimed it and the trust conditions touch drift, correctness, enforcement, and recovery all at once, more ground than one role can watch alone.

Ask who owns deciding which guarantees are hard walls the system physically cannot cross, versus soft instructions the agent is merely asked to follow. That one belongs to the architect, in principle. In practice it is frequently a decision nobody made explicitly, so the system defaults to whichever is easier to build, which is usually the soft version.

Ask who builds the guardrails, the monitoring, the path back when something goes wrong. Ownership here tends to be split across an architect and an operations function, each assuming the other has it further covered than they do.

And ask who owns watching the thing once it runs, on a standing basis, day after day, this is the cell that comes up blank most often. Not thinly staffed. Blank. There is a well-understood job description and a market rate for the engineer who builds an agent. There is rarely an equivalent for the person whose job is to watch it run.

Put the two rows side by side and the shape is consistent across teams: a full top row and a bottom row that is half-staffed at best, with the rightmost cell, ongoing supervision, empty more often than not.

Where the money actually gets lost

The empty column would be a tolerable inefficiency if the failures it produces were minor. They are not, and the reason is that the costliest agentic failures rarely live inside a single cell. They live in the seam between two cells, in the hand-off where one person's job ends and nobody has confirmed whose job starts next.

Here is the shape of it, stripped of any particular vendor or system. A product manager specifies a boundary: this agent may act on its own up to a certain point, and beyond that point it must stop and ask. That is an intent-level decision, a Channel 2 cell. Somewhere downstream, an engineer builds the agent to operate inside that boundary, a Channel 1 cell. The two cells look adjacent on the grid. They are not the same cell, and the confirmation that the boundary was actually built as a hard stop, not just described in a document, is a separate piece of work that sits in the seam between them.

On a team without a named owner for that seam, everyone can be doing their job correctly and the boundary still doesn't hold. The product manager wrote the specification. The engineer built to the specification as understood. The gap is the part that was never anyone's explicit responsibility: verifying that what was specified is what got enforced. When that agent later takes an action past the line it was supposed to respect, the postmortem finds three people who each did their part and no one who owned the connection between the parts.

That is not a hypothetical edge case. It is the general shape of how agentic failures happen, because an agent that decides and acts on its own, rather than following a fixed set of rules, will eventually meet a situation the specification did not anticipate, and what happens next depends entirely on whether the hand-offs around it were owned or assumed.

Why the column stays empty

It is tempting to treat this as a staffing gap, the kind of thing solved by posting one more job requisition. That undersells what is actually missing.

The old model knows how to add a seat. Across a generation of reorganizations it absorbed new kinds of judgment one chair at a time, and each addition slotted into the existing row without disturbing it. Channel 2 is not one more chair in that row. It is an entire second row, with its own intent, its own structure, its own build, its own operate. You cannot hire a second pipeline the way you hire a fourth teammate, because a pipeline first has to be recognized as existing before anyone can be assigned to own a piece of it.

That is the honest answer to why the column stays empty even on capable, well-resourced teams. They are staffing the model they can see. The model they can see has one row. The second row is not being neglected; it is invisible to a picture of the work that never had a place for it. You do not forget to fill a cell you can see sitting empty. You fail to fill a cell your map of the work never drew.

Drawing the grid on your own product

Here is the exercise, and any team can run it in an hour with nothing more than a whiteboard and the willingness to be honest about the answers. Draw the two rows and four columns. For each of the top-row cells, write the name of the person who owns it. For each of the bottom-row cells, do the same.

Most teams get through the top row without hesitation. The bottom row is where the conversation slows down, and the slowing down is the finding. Someone will say "I think that's on the platform team," and someone else will say "I thought that was yours," and that exchange, uncomfortable as it is in the room, is worth more than the exercise that produced it, because it surfaces the assumed owner before an incident does it for you.

Then go one step further and ask, for every boundary between a top-row cell and its corresponding bottom-row cell, who confirms the hand-off actually happened. Not who is responsible for the work on either side. Who checks that what one side specified is what the other side built. That question, asked out loud in a room, is usually the first time anyone has asked it.

What the empty cell is quietly waiting for

None of this requires abandoning the team you have. The product manager, architect, engineer, and operations lead are not being replaced by this grid. They are being asked to notice that a second set of questions has been sitting next to their familiar ones the entire time, unassigned, waiting for someone to claim it.

The grid does not tell you who those people should be. It only tells you that the cells exist, whether or not anyone is sitting in them. A team that draws the grid without flinching and finds its bottom row empty has not discovered a flaw in its people. It has discovered the shape of the work it has been doing without realizing half of it was still undone, and the next question, the one that actually decides whether the empty column gets filled or stays that way through the next incident, is who in the room is willing to say the cell is theirs.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the collaboration grid and the hand-offs where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Failure No One Is Watching
entry.content:
The Failure No One Is Watching

Post 8, The Agentic AI Team series

Eighteen months after an agentic product ships is the moment it is most likely to be quietly broken and least likely to be caught. Not because anyone stopped watching. Because everyone kept watching the same thing.

Picture a generic enterprise agent that triages support tickets and drafts responses for a human to send. It launched to strong numbers: fast resolution, high approval rates on its drafts. Eighteen months later, the dashboard still looks the same, resolution time still fast, approval rate still high, and the agent is quietly worse at its job than it was at launch, in a way none of those numbers was built to catch.

That gap, between a dashboard that reads healthy and a product that is not, is not an ordinary monitoring failure. It is the cleanest proof that supervision cannot rest on one seat, because the ways an agentic product degrades are, by their nature, spread across sightlines no single seat holds.

What "silent" actually means here

Start with what the failure looks like, because it does not look like a failure. The agent keeps producing fluent, confident output. Nothing throws an error. No alert fires. The team's instruments stay green, because the instruments measure what they were built to measure at launch, and the agent is still doing that.

Underneath, its real performance has been sliding for months, along a path no instrument on the team is pointed at. This is not a hypothetical concern. Predictive models deployed at scale in other high-stakes domains have been measured, years into production, performing far below their marketed accuracy, with nobody catching it until an outside party ran the numbers out of curiosity rather than obligation. No regulator required the check. No vendor volunteered it. The mechanism that should have caught it did not exist.

Call this condition silent degradation: a deployed agent producing confident output while its real performance decays along several axes at once, none of them caught by the instruments built at launch. It has no settled name in most product organizations yet, which is part of why so many agentic deployments will meet it and not recognize it.

Six ways to drift, six different people who would have to notice

The reason silent degradation survives so long is that it is not one failure with one owner. It is several independent failures compounding at once, and each one belongs to a different role.

The first is attentional. The person supervising the agent gets worse at supervising it precisely because the agent is reliable. Vigilance erodes with success; the ticket-triage agent that has been right for a year gets a lighter and lighter read from the human checking its drafts. That is the supervisor's vector, and the supervisor is inside the very decay they are supposed to catch.

The second is substrate. The ground the agent stands on moves on several axes independently: the underlying model gets updated by a vendor, the knowledge base it retrieves from goes stale, an API it calls changes behavior, a system prompt accumulates small edits tested alone and never together. Each shift is small. Together they compound. This vector belongs to the architect, who knows what the system is built on, and to whoever owns evaluation, who could measure the shift if asked to re-run it.

The third is observational, and it is the strangest, because the thing decaying is the detection system itself. The engineer who built the original dashboard moves to another team, taking with them the reason a particular threshold was chosen. The dashboard from month two still runs in month eighteen, and nobody remembers what it was for. This belongs to production operations and to a team's institutional memory, precisely the thing an organization loses as people rotate.

The fourth is compensatory, and it is the reason a degrading agent can look healthy for a year and a half. Support agents using the tool learn its blind spots. They stop trusting its drafts on a certain category of ticket and quietly rewrite those by hand. Their own metric, did the ticket get closed, stays green. The system's metric, was the agent's draft actually useful, was never separately measured. What is actually holding the product up is a shadow workflow the users built without telling anyone. The people who can see this are the domain experts and the users doing the compensating, and they are rarely in the room when the agent's health gets reviewed.

The fifth is security. The agent that was safe against manipulation at launch faces an attacker population that has since learned its boundaries, found the prompts that work against it, and shared them. The agent did not get weaker. The threat environment got better at exploiting it. A red-team report from launch day is a snapshot of a target that has since moved, and this vector belongs to security, which on most teams tested the agent once before launch and has not been back since.

The sixth is scope, and it is the one a product manager is most likely to stumble onto by accident. The agent shipped to triage and draft. Over time, a well-meaning adjustment lets it also auto-send replies for a narrow category of routine ticket, because that one exception saved someone an afternoon. Then another exception. Six months on, the agent is closing categories of tickets on its own that nobody formally decided it should handle, and the answer to "who approved this" is a chain of small accommodations rather than a decision. This vector belongs to the product manager, who owns the mandate, and the architect, who owns the tool permissions that would need to be pulled back.

Lay the six side by side and the shape of the problem becomes obvious: at least five distinct owners, and not one of them can see the other five. The degradation is real, it is often large, and it is invisible not because anyone hid it but because it is distributed, smeared across sightlines such that no single sightline contains it.

Why the dashboards agree with each other and are all wrong

This is the part worth sitting with, because it explains why competent people miss this for so long. Each dashboard was built by a competent person to catch a specific, narrower thing, and each one is still doing exactly that job. The resolution-time chart is correctly reporting resolution time. The approval-rate chart is correctly reporting approval rate. Nobody's instrument is lying. The failure is that the instruments were each built to answer one question, and the composite question, is this agent still doing what we think it is doing, was never assigned to anyone.

A product manager watching this agent can see the scope vector, because scope happens to sit in a product manager's normal field of view. They cannot measure the supervisor's eroding attention; that is a human-factors problem. They cannot detect a silent model update; that is the architect's terrain. They cannot see the shadow workflow the support team quietly built; that lives with the users. They cannot re-run the red team; that is security's calendar. A product manager doing everything right, by the standards of their own seat, will still see only one face of a six-faced problem and reasonably call the agent healthy on the strength of that one face.

That is the proof, not an illustration of it. The six vectors live in separate domains of expertise: human factors, infrastructure, institutional memory, domain judgment, adversarial security, product scope. A failure that lives across six domains cannot be caught by strengthening any one of them. It can only be caught by a structure that puts all six sightlines in the same room on a schedule.

What watching the running agent actually requires

None of this argues for a smarter dashboard. It argues for a different kind of ownership, one that does not exist on the classic three-seat team and has to be built on purpose.

Each vector needs a named owner, not a shared assumption that someone is handling it. The instruments themselves need a version and a refresh cadence, because an instrument calibrated against the agent's launch-day behavior is measuring an agent that no longer exists a year later. Someone has to own that recalibration on a calendar, not as a favor squeezed in when there is spare time.

And someone has to own the composite. This is a role whose job is not to watch the agent do its work, but to watch the six people who each watch one slice of it, and to notice when their reports stop adding up to a coherent picture. Call it the AgentOps seat, or the supervisor of supervisors, or whatever a given organization wants to name it. What matters is that it exists as a job someone is accountable for, not a hope that the six owners will happen to compare notes on their own. On most teams today, when you ask who holds that seat, the answer is no one. That is not a staffing gap to fill quietly. It is the empty column the whole industry is still treating as a rounding error.

Finally, at intervals, someone has to bring in a check with no stake in the answer: not the vendor who built the model, not the internal team whose bonus depends on the dashboard staying green, but an outside party measuring real performance against a sample it did not help design. That is how the failures in other industries eventually surfaced, and it took years each time because nobody was required to look. An agentic product does not get to wait for a curious outsider to stumble onto the problem. Somebody inside has to have already asked the six questions first.

Go back to the ticket-triage agent at month eighteen. Every dashboard the team built is still green, and every one of them was built to look at exactly one slice of the problem, never at whether the other five slices still agreed with it. Pull up your own agent's dashboards right now, and ask, for each of the six vectors, who last looked. If the answer for even one of them is no one, you already know which direction the agent has been drifting, and for how long nobody has noticed.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines why silent degradation is the cleanest proof that an agentic product cannot be supervised from a single seat. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Architecture That Doesn't Exist Yet Is a Team
entry.content:
The Architecture That Doesn't Exist Yet Is a Team

Post 11, The Agentic AI Team series

A claims agent denies a man's insulin reimbursement after a refrigeration outage. Nothing about the denial breaks a rule. The exclusion is ambiguous, the agent reads it the way that closes the claim fastest, and every wall the team built holds while it does. No policy is violated. No guardrail fires. The groceries get paid. The insulin does not, and the man will never know an agent decided this, only that the answer was no and that appealing it is an ordeal he cannot afford in a month he is already short on money.

That is the case this series has been circling since the first post, in different clothes each time. A procurement agent placing an order against a contract nobody told it was gone. A support agent staying calm while a customer describes real harm. A fleet of agents drifting the same cheap habit into ten thousand decisions nobody is watching one at a time. Ten posts, ten roles, one repeated discovery: the failure is never inside a single person's work. It is in the gap between the people, the place where each side assumed the other had it.

This is the last post in the series, and it owes you the plainest version of what all of it was for.

Where runtime ownership actually lives

The thesis running under this whole series is that an agentic product is two products. The agent that decides and acts, and the layer that supervises it: the boundary on what it may do alone, the moment a consequential action pauses for a person, the record of what it did and why, the path back when it did something wrong. Call them Channel 1 and Channel 2. Every team knows how to build the first. Almost none of them staff the second, because it is invisible when it works and catastrophic only on the morning it doesn't.

The nine posts between the first and this one went looking for who owns Channel 2, one seat at a time. The architect who enforces the boundary rather than merely documenting it. The eval owner who can say whether the agent is still correct, not just still running. The AgentOps supervisor whose actual job is watching the fleet as it drifts. The domain expert who knows what a wrong answer costs and who pays it. Each of those seats is real, and each of them closes part of the gap the three-seat team never had a chair for.

But watch what happens when you line all of them up against the insulin claim. The boundary held. The eval suite passed. The monitoring showed nothing unusual, because a curt, technically correct denial does not trip an anomaly detector. Every seat did its job, and the man still lost his claim. The failure was not in any surface. It was in the space between the surfaces, in the vast, ordinary territory of actions that are available and not destructive, where an agent can be a bad actor without ever touching a rule that says it can't.

That is the seam this whole series has been pointing at. Failures do not live in the roles. They live in what the roles, even done well, leave uncovered between them.

Stated is not enforced, one more time

There is a document a lot of teams reach for once they notice this gap: a values statement for the agent, a paragraph about fairness and honesty shipped into the system prompt. It reads well. It changes nothing.

A prompt is advisory. On the run where the inputs line up wrong, the agent reasons past it anyway. A policy that no system enforces is the same prompt with better formatting. A guardrail is the first thing in the stack that actually binds, because it sits in the execution path and makes an action structurally unavailable, but a guardrail can only express a prohibition. It can block a denial above a dollar limit. It cannot make an agent behave well in the enormous space of actions that are legal, available, and quietly wrong.

What actually governs what kind of actor an agent is, this series has been calling its constitution, and the word needs the same warning constitutions always need. It is not the document. It is the enforcement around the document: the values wired into evaluation cases that test whether the agent honors them under pressure, into monitoring that watches for the values eroding over months, into escalation rules that fire when a case crosses a line a human, not the agent, should decide. A constitution you can only state is a wish with good vocabulary. A constitution you can enforce is a discipline, provable in a table with a row per commitment: what enforces it, what eval case tests it, what signal would show it decaying, and who owns that row.

The rule that ran under every post in this series is the same rule that runs under the constitution. Stated is not enforced. It was true of the autonomy boundary in post one. It is true of the values on the last page.

Why no single seat can hold this

Here is where the field keeps making its most understandable mistake. Faced with a gap this wide, the instinct is to close it by naming an owner, usually the product manager, and handing them a mandate broad enough to cover it: you are the one who makes sure this agent behaves. It is a generous instruction and an impossible one, and it fails for a reason that has nothing to do with effort.

One person is one set of blind spots. The values a constitution encodes belong to the domain, to the product, to the person the agent's decision lands on, and no single seat sees all three at once. The enforcement that makes those values bind is an engineering discipline. The proof that the enforcement is still working is a measurement discipline. The judgment about when the world has shifted enough that the values themselves need revisiting is an institutional one, slower than any single sprint. Stack all of that on one seat and you have not built a safeguard. You have built a single sightline standing in for a problem that was designed, by its own nature, to defeat single sightlines.

This is also why the fix is not a bigger platform. Enterprise agent infrastructure is getting more capable every quarter: escalation hooks, evaluation harnesses, drift monitors, tool-scoping controls, more of it shipping all the time. A team that ignores these tools is choosing to hand-build what it could buy. But the platform has a floor it will never cross, because it can ship the apparatus that enforces a value and it cannot ship the value itself. No vendor knows whether this exclusion is too ambiguous to deny on, what fairness means for this claimant, when the metric should yield to the person in front of it. That judgment is the product's and the domain's, and it stays a team's work no matter how far the infrastructure matures, moving up through hand-built artifacts, into a consolidated control plane, and eventually into a platform primitive, but never disappearing into the box.

The re-vantage this whole series has been building toward

An earlier framing of this problem, the one many product leaders have already absorbed, put the weight of all of it on one seat. In the window before the supervisory layer exists as real infrastructure, the argument went, the product manager has to be that foundation personally. Build it well, because the people downstream of your agent are depending on you.

That framing was honest about the gap. It was wrong about where the load-bearing wall goes.

The architecture that does not exist yet is not a person. It is a team, and not in the loose sense of people who share a standup, but in the exact sense this series has meant it from post one: a set of distinct judgments, held by distinct seats, arranged so that what none of them could see alone, all of them together can. The boundary and the person who enforces it. The checkmark and the person who can prove it still means something. The values and the person who keeps them current as the domain moves under them. The running agent and the human who still, somewhere up the stack, answers for it.

That is the turn the whole series has been assembling, ten posts at a time. Not that the three-seat team was wrong, but that it was half a team for a job that now has two halves, and the second half does not get staffed by asking harder of the first.

A team-design checklist for one agentic product

Before you ship, or before you audit what is already live, run one product against this list. It is short on purpose. A checklist that tries to cover every case gets skipped; this one only asks whether the ownership itself is real.

  1. Name who owns the autonomy boundary, and confirm it is enforced in code, not described in a document a human is trusted to remember under pressure.
  2. Name who owns the approval moment, the point where a consequential action pauses for a person, and confirm that person is shown enough to actually decide, not a confirmation button in front of a decision already made.
  3. Name who owns the audit surface, and confirm it captures why the agent acted, not only that it acted, in a form that would survive being read by someone outside the team.
  4. Name who owns the recovery workflow, and confirm it was designed in daylight, not left for whoever is on call the night it is needed.
  5. Name who watches the running agent, on a cadence that assumes attention erodes precisely because the agent has been reliable, and confirm that watch does not quietly stop the month nothing goes wrong.
  6. Name who owns the constitution's values, separately from who enforces them, separately from who can prove the enforcement is still holding, and confirm those are not all the same busy person by default.
  7. Name who owns the empty column, the seam between two seats where each assumed the other had it, and confirm that name was chosen on purpose and not discovered during the incident.

Run this against one product you actually have. If more than two of those seven answers are "no one, really," you have not found a smaller version of the problem. You have found the same one, at your own scale, still waiting.

The team you have and the team the work needs

Look back at the man with the insulin claim. Nobody on the team that built Nemo was careless. The boundary held, the eval suite ran, the monitoring watched for the things it was built to watch for. What was missing was never a person working harder. It was a seat that did not exist, holding a judgment none of the seats that did exist were positioned to make.

That is the condition most agentic products are shipping in right now, not because the teams building them are behind, but because the shape of the team has not caught up to the shape of the thing it built. The three seats that built a generation of software were the correct team for software a human drives. They are still the correct team for that. They were never going to be the correct team for something that decides on its own, in cases nobody specified, long after it has left anyone's hands.

So the question this series leaves you with is not whether your agent works. Most of them do, for months, which is exactly what makes the gap so easy to miss. The question is whether the team standing behind it is the team the three-seat era handed you, quietly stretched to cover a second product it was never built for, or whether it is the team this one actually needs. You already know which one you have. The harder question is whether you have said so out loud, to the people who could still do something about it before the next claim is the one that finds the seam.

This post closes The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: Autonomous Diagnostics on Kyma Unplugged: Goose + LiteLLM + KRO + SAP AI Core in Production
entry.content:
Autonomous Cluster Diagnostics on Kyma Unplugged with Goose + LiteLLM + KRO + SAP AI Core in Production

As of 2026-07. Model versions and provider catalog reflect SAP AI Core availability at time of writing.

Modern Kubernetes operations need more than dashboards. They need reasoning.

This post describes a fully in-cluster autonomous diagnostic pipeline running on Kyma Unplugged:

  • a Goose AI agent with live kubectl and kyma CLI access
  • investigating anomalies adaptively
  • persisting structured findings to Kubernetes custom resources visible in Busola
  • streaming spend telemetry to SAP Cloud Logging via Kyma's built-in metric pipeline

No external calls at runtime. One terraform apply to deploy.

Stack

  • Kyma Unplugged (open-source Kyma; also runs on SAP BTP Kyma Runtime)
  • Goose AI — adaptive agent with shell tool access
  • LiteLLM proxy — routes to SAP AI Core (Claude Sonnet/Opus, GPT-5.5, Mistral, Nova via Bedrock)
  • Node.js llmproxy — bridges to SAP Orchestration V2 to enable prompt caching
  • KRO Resource Group Definitions — the entire deployment as a single declarative unit
  • Two parallel CronJobs — one static (v1, every 10 min), one adaptive (v2, daily)
  • Kyma Telemetry MetricPipeline → SAP Cloud Logging (OpenSearch) with a shipped dashboard

Custom resources produced

  • ClusterHealthReport — 12-section structured health analysis
  • AuditLogCatalog — security and configuration change audit
  • FleetStatus — aggregated fleet-wide health with live spend

Findings from production runs

  • 402 ingress gateway revisions accumulated in five days (unbounded revision history)
  • 18 leftover cluster-admin bindings from development tooling
  • An Istio EnvoyFilter silently blocking operator reconciliation for weeks
  • cert-manager ACME solver stuck for 5+ days on a DNS zone selector mismatch

Cost, approximate

  • ~$1.10 per adaptive v2 run
  • ~$0.004 per v1 static run when prompt caching is active on the Orchestration V2 path

Why an AI diagnostic pipeline?

Static probes tell you a pod is running. They don't tell you that an EnvoyFilter installed three weeks ago is silently intercepting reconciliation traffic. Or that cert-manager's ACME solver has a network policy gap waiting to surface at the next certificate renewal. Or that Istio has quietly accumulated 402 ingress gateway revisions in etcd since Tuesday.

Finding those needs correlation, inspection, and reasoning about blast radius — exactly what a language model with live tool access is built for.

So we put one inside the cluster. Not on someone's laptop, not as an ephemeral shell session — a scheduled, autonomous, in-cluster pipeline writing structured Kubernetes resources.

 

Architecture

The stack breaks into five layers:

  1. AI agent — Goose
  2. Gateway — OpenClaw + LiteLLM + llmproxy
  3. Backend — SAP AI Core
  4. Persistence — KRO CRDs + PostgreSQL
  5. Observability — Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging

At a glance:

 

graph TD
  A[AI Agent<br/>Goose] --> G[Gateway<br/>OpenClaw · LiteLLM · llmproxy]
  G --> B[Backend<br/>SAP AI Core]
  A --> P[Persistence<br/>KRO CRDs · PostgreSQL]
  A --> O[Observability<br/>Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging]

And in full:

 

graph TD
  subgraph Kyma Unplugged
    CJ1[CronJob: diagnose-v1<br/>every 10 min]
    CJ2[CronJob: diagnose-v2<br/>daily]

    subgraph diagnose-v2 Pod
      ORCH[Container: diagnose-v2<br/>orchestrator]
      GH[Container: goose-v2-health<br/>12-section analysis]
      GA[Container: goose-v2-audit<br/>security audit]
    end

    CJ1 -->|orch/ model prefix| LLMP[Node.js llmproxy :4000<br/>Orchestration V2 + cache_control]
    CJ2 --> ORCH
    ORCH -->|parallel exec| GH
    ORCH -->|parallel exec| GA

    GH -->|kubectl / kyma CLI| K8S[(Kubernetes API)]
    GA -->|kubectl / kyma CLI| K8S

    GH --> LLP[LiteLLM Proxy :4001]
    GA --> LLP

    LLMP -->|Orchestration V2| AICORE
    LLP -->|sap/ provider| AICORE[SAP AI Core<br/>Claude · GPT · Mistral · Nova]
    LLP -->|cost tracking| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    LLMP -->|cost tracking| PG

    PUSHGW[Pushgateway] <-->|push accurate spend| CJ1
    LLP -->|/metrics<br/>pod annotations| KYMA_TEL[Kyma Telemetry<br/>metric-agent]
    PUSHGW -->|/metrics<br/>pod annotations| KYMA_TEL
    KYMA_TEL -->|MetricPipeline · OTLP| CLS[SAP Cloud Logging<br/>OpenSearch]

    GH -->|write| CHR[ClusterHealthReport CRD]
    GA -->|write| ALC[AuditLogCatalog CRD]

    OC[OpenClaw<br/>AI Gateway] --> LLP
    CHR -->|Busola extension| UI[Busola UI]
    ALC -->|Busola extension| UI
  end

Design principle: every component is a KRO Resource Group Definition. One terraform apply provisions the whole stack.

Two pipelines: static and adaptive

v1 — static (every 10 minutes). kyma alpha diagnose produces a ~51k-token cluster snapshot. Goose consumes it as a static blob and emits a structured summary. Fast, cheap, catches regressions between runs.

v2 — adaptive (daily). Goose gets direct kubectl and kyma access via the built-in developer extension and investigates across 12 sections:

# Section What Goose checks
1Health StatusCluster + Kyma module conditions
2IssuesDegraded resources, error events
3Kyma ModulesModule reconciliation state
4Impact AssessmentBlast radius of active issues
5Node ResourcesCPU/memory pressure, disk, conditions
6Workload HealthDeployments, statefulsets, daemonsets
7Security AssessmentRBAC surface, privileged workloads
8Drift DetectionResources diverging from declared state
9Cost AttributionNamespace resource consumption estimates
10Network Policy AuditConnectivity gaps, permissive policies
11Certificate Expiry ForecastCerts within 30 days of expiry
12Recent EventsHigh-frequency warning events in last hour

The v2 pod runs three containers concurrently — orchestrator, health worker, audit worker. Total job time is bounded by the longer worker (~230s health), roughly a third faster than running them sequentially.

LiteLLM proxy + SAP AI Core

The LiteLLM proxy runs as a Deployment on port 4001. The sap/ provider prefix routes to SAP AI Core using an OAuth2-obtained token, with the service key assembled from individual Kubernetes secret keys at startup.

What SAP AI Core actually is

SAP AI Core is not "OpenAI or Anthropic under a different URL." It is a proprietary orchestration layer — SAP's LLM Module — that fronts multiple backend providers:

 
OpenClaw / Goose v2
   │ openai-completions
   ▼
LiteLLM Proxy :4001
   │ sap/ provider + OAuth2 token
   ▼
SAP AI Core LLM Module
   ├──▶ AWS Bedrock (Claude Opus/Sonnet)
   ├──▶ Azure OpenAI (GPT-5.5, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1)
   └──▶ Mistral (mistral-large, mistral-small)

Behavioural notes from running this in production:

  • Reasoning traces don't come back on the foundation-model inference path. reasoning: true is accepted, but no reasoning_content appears in the response. SAP's LLM Module filters it server-side.
  • Vision works on all non-Mistral models. Images must be base64-encoded; public URLs are rejected.
  • drop_params: true is required. SAP AI Core rejects unknown parameters; this tells LiteLLM to drop them silently rather than fail. Do not turn it off.

Consequence of drop_params: true. Any parameter or beta header SAP doesn't recognize is silently dropped — including some Anthropic beta flags. "Accepted" doesn't mean "activated." If you rely on a beta feature (extended output, prompt caching, thinking blocks), assume it's off until you can see evidence in the response payload or in Postgres.

Production glue: the no_stream_on_tools hook

Three problems solved in a single LiteLLM pre-call hook:

  1. Disable streaming when tools are present — Claude Opus returns malformed streamed tool-call responses through SAP.
  2. Strip SAP Orchestration template placeholders from message content — they cause 400: Unused parameters: ['placeholder'] on the inference endpoint.
  3. Flatten pure-text content lists ([{"type":"text","text":"..."}]) to plain strings — the inference endpoint rejects them as 400: Unused parameters: ['text'].

Sketch of the content normalization:

 
def _normalize_content(content):
    if isinstance(content, str):
        return _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', content)
    if isinstance(content, list):
        all_text = all(
            isinstance(b, dict) and b.get('type') == 'text' for b in content
        )
        if all_text:
            return ' '.join(
                _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b.get('text', '')) for b in content
            )
        # mixed content (e.g. vision): strip placeholders from text blocks only
        return [
            {**b, 'text': _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b['text'])}
              if b.get('type') == 'text' else b
            for b in content
        ]
    return content
 
_SAP_TEMPLATE_RE = re.compile(r'\{\{\s*\?[^}]*\}\}')

That regex matches SAP's Orchestration placeholder syntax ({{?placeholder_name}}) that occasionally leaks into inference-path payloads.

Model tier aliases

Callers reference tiers, not specific models. Backing models can rotate centrally without touching every deployment:

  • reasoning-fast → SAP-hosted Claude Sonnet (default; used by both Goose v1 and v2)
  • reasoning-hard → SAP-hosted Claude Opus (deep reasoning; roughly an order of magnitude more expensive per call on our workload)

128k output on Claude

Configure max_tokens: 64000 together with extra_headers: {"anthropic-beta": "output-128k-2025-02-19"} to unlock 128k output on Claude. Goose v2 health reports regularly land above 17k tokens — well past the default 8k cap — which is circumstantial evidence the header is being honoured. Because drop_params: true can silently swallow beta headers SAP doesn't recognize, treat that as a working configuration to periodically re-verify rather than a settled guarantee.

SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching

The biggest cost lever we identified is prompt caching, and it lives on a different SAP AI Core API surface than the sap/ LiteLLM provider uses:

 

TypescenarioIdEndpointPayload
Foundation model inferencefoundation-models/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/chat/completionsOpenAI-compatible
Orchestrationorchestration/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/v2/completionSAP modules schema

The sap/ provider hits the foundation-model path. Prompt caching is designed to be exposed via Orchestration V2. To use it without rewriting every agent, we run a Node.js llmproxy next to LiteLLM:

 
Goose v1 (orch/ prefix)
   │
   ▼
Node.js llmproxy :4000
   │ detect orch/ prefix by deployment scenarioId
   │ convert OpenAI messages → V2 prompt_templating format
   │ inject cache_control: ephemeral on system + last user message
   │ strip cache_control from tools (SAP V2 rejects it there)
   │ extract final_result from V2 response
   ▼
SAP AI Core Orchestration V2

Implementation gotchas worth knowing:

  • scenarioId: "orchestration" on the deployment is the routing key — the only reliable way to distinguish orchestration from foundation-model deployments.
  • /v2 appears twice in the path: {AI_API_URL}/v2/inference/deployments/{id}/v2/completion.
  • cache_control on tools is rejected. Goose (correctly, per Anthropic spec) attaches cache_control at the top-level tool object; SAP's V2 schema rejects it there with 400: Additional properties not allowed. Strip it before forwarding.
  • Response wrapping. V2 returns final_result.choices, not top-level choices. Unwrap before returning to the caller.

Verifying caching is actually happening. Configuration alone isn't proof — SAP's LLM Module has a track record of reshaping payloads quietly. In our own smoke tests the llmproxy logs confirm routing via the V2 orchestration path with no cache_control errors, but cached_tokens: 0 came back on both calls of a short (~18-token) system prompt — too small to be a valid cache candidate. Verification at production scale (Goose v1's ~17k-token system prompt) is still on our to-do list. If caching matters for your budget, confirm by inspecting usage.cache_creation_input_tokens and usage.cache_read_input_tokens on V2 responses, or by watching the per-request cost delta in Postgres across repeat calls with a stable system prompt.

If the V2 path preserves caching end-to-end, v1's ~17k-token system prompt is written once and served from cache thereafter — the intended architecture behind the low per-call cost target for repeat v1 runs.

Observability: /metrics → Kyma Telemetry → SAP Cloud Logging

This is the piece that makes the whole stack operable, and it's cleaner than it has any right to be. No manual ServiceMonitor, no Prometheus deployment, no custom scrape config. Kyma Telemetry's metric-agent DaemonSet does the scraping natively via pod annotations, and a single MetricPipeline custom resource ships everything to SAP Cloud Logging over OTLP.

The pod-annotation contract

Any pod with these annotations gets scraped automatically:

 
metadata:
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port:   "4001"
    prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

That's the whole integration surface. Add the annotations to the LiteLLM Deployment and the Pushgateway; the metric agent picks them up on next reconciliation, scrapes /metrics on the specified port, wraps the samples as OTLP, and pushes them to the pipeline output.

The MetricPipeline resource

One CR describes where metrics go:

 
apiVersion: telemetry.kyma-project.io/v1beta1
kind: MetricPipeline
metadata:
  name: cloud-logging
spec:
  input:
    prometheus:
      enabled: true            # scrape annotated pods
  output:
    otlp:
      endpoint:
        valueFrom:
          secretKeyRef:
            name: sap-cloud-logging
            namespace: kyma-system
            key: ingest-otlp-endpoint

No agents to deploy, no scrape rules to maintain — Kyma Telemetry handles both scraping and shipping. Everything lands in SAP Cloud Logging's OpenSearch index metrics-otel-v1-*.

LiteLLM /metrics — the two config gotchas

Two settings in litellm_settings (not general_settings — the docs disagree with themselves; the correct section is litellm_settings😞

 
litellm_settings:
  callbacks: ["prometheus"]
  require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: false

The first enables the Prometheus callback so /metrics is populated. The second removes auth from that endpoint so the metric agent can scrape it in-cluster without an API key.

Layered spend telemetry

The metric-agent path handles counters (rate-of-spend, latency, request counts) — great for trends but subject to reset on pod restart. For accurate absolute totals, a Pushgateway holds gauges that are refreshed from Postgres every 10 minutes by the v1 CronJob (litellm_actual_spend_usd, litellm_actual_tokens, litellm_actual_calls per model + cluster). The metric agent scrapes the Pushgateway with the same annotation pattern, so the pipeline is uniform.

Sources of truth:

  • Trend / real-time signal — LiteLLM counters via Kyma Telemetry
  • Accurate daily totals — Pushgateway gauges backed by Postgres
  • FleetStatus CRD — same accurate figures rendered in Busola per cluster
  • PostgreSQL — per-request detail; user-agent field distinguishes Goose (Goose/*) from OpenClaw interactive sessions (OpenAI/JS*)

The shipped OpenSearch dashboard

litellm-spend-dashboard.ndjson imports a ready-made dashboard into SAP Cloud Logging with 10 panels covering the operational questions you actually ask on Monday morning:

  • Per-model spend donut — where the money went
  • Spend over time, split by cluster — which cluster is bleeding
  • Goose vs interactive split — automation cost vs human cost
  • Latency by model — surface backend slowdowns
  • Token throughput and call volume — capacity signals
  • Cache-read vs cache-creation tokens (when caching is confirmed active) — how much prompt caching actually saved
  • Error rate by model — SAP-side hiccups, provider-side hiccups

Import once, and every cluster's telemetry shows up under the same views.

 

Cost and cadence

 The daily llm spend and cluster health overview is in busola landing page

 


ComponentModelApproximate costRun cadence
v1 static health (via Orchestration V2)orch/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet~$0.004 (target, with caching active)¹every 10 min (Goose every 12h)
v2 adaptive healthsap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet~$1.10daily 03:07 UTC
v2 adaptive auditsap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnetincluded abovedaily 03:07 UTC

Why the different prefixes? v1 uses orch/ because it routes through llmproxy for SAP Orchestration V2 (where prompt caching lives). v2 uses sap/ because it goes straight through LiteLLM. Same underlying model, different API surface — see the SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching section for the routing detail.

¹ v1 cost target assumes prompt caching is honored end-to-end on the Orchestration V2 path; production-scale verification is still pending — see the caching section.

Model routing. Goose v1 and v2 pin claude-4.5-sonnet directly via GOOSE_MODEL in the RGD (not through the reasoning-fast tier alias) so the diagnostic pipeline is independent of central tier rotation. OpenClaw interactive sessions default to reasoning-hard (Claude Opus). In the SAP Cloud Logging dashboard: sonnet traffic ≈ Goose diagnostics; opus traffic ≈ interactive user sessions. Per-model pricing and the authoritative model catalog live in SAP Note 3437766 (S-user login required).

Cadence evolution. v2 started at 30-minute intervals — around $150/day per cluster at that rate. Dropping to daily plus enabling caching on the v1 Orchestration V2 path is what turns the numbers around; we now run v1 Goose every 12 hours (gooseEvery=72) instead of once a month, and the total bill dropped substantially in observed Postgres totals.

GOOSE_MAX_TOKENS=16000 and GOOSE_CONTEXT_LIMIT=100000 cap per-response output and total context. With --max-turns 30, a typical v2 job completes in ~3 minutes.

Findings persist as Kubernetes CRDs with Busola custom extensions:

  • ClusterHealthReport — 12-panel detail view, CodeViewer per section
  • AuditLogCatalog — searchable tables of failed operations and config changes
  • FleetStatus — aggregated fleet card with live spend, AI Core catalog counts, and audit stats

Raw Goose working transcripts are archived to rolling ConfigMaps (goose-raw-<timestamp>, last 6 retained) for post-incident review.

 

Findings from production

Runs on development Kyma clusters surfaced issues static monitoring missed:

  • 402 ingress gateway revisions in 5 days. Istio revision history was unbounded; ReplicaSets accumulated and consumed etcd space. Root cause: a deployment with imagePullPolicy: Always cycling every 17 minutes.
  • 18 cluster-admin bindings. Leftover ClusterRoleBinding resources from dev tooling, still bound to unused service accounts. Goose ran kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=system:serviceaccount:default:dev-toolbox -A to confirm blast radius.
  • EnvoyFilter blocking reconciliation. An EnvoyFilter for SAP BTP Connectivity Proxy was intercepting traffic on a port the Istio operator uses. Reconciliation silently failed for weeks. Goose correlated the filter's selector labels against operator error messages to identify it.
  • cert-manager ACME solver gap. Three certificates stuck pending for 5+ days on a DNS zone selector mismatch in the ClusterIssuer. Goose produced the exact kubectl edit clusterissuer fix.

What's next

  • Reasoning traces. SAP's LLM Module strips Claude thinking blocks on the foundation-model path. When they're preserved (via Orchestration V2 or otherwise), OpenClaw's /reasoning toggle will surface chain-of-thought from in-cluster diagnostics.
  • Scoped remediation. Goose already has kubectl. Next: a tightly scoped set of approved remediation actions (unstuck finalizers, restart crashlooping pods) with every action written to AuditLogCatalog.
  • Cross-cluster comparison. Same pipeline across multiple Kyma instances, using spend telemetry to flag clusters with anomalous resource consumption for their workload profile.
  • On-call assistant. Raw transcript ConfigMaps already make past diagnostic runs searchable context. Next step is a Goose skill in OpenClaw's chat for ad-hoc cluster queries.

Full stack in a single KRO RGD. One terraform apply. No external runtime dependencies. That constraint — everything in-cluster, everything declarative, everything observable through Kyma's own pipeline — turned out to be the most consequential architectural decision of the whole system.

Stack: Goose AI · LiteLLM · SAP AI Core · KRO · Kyma Unplugged · SAP Cloud Logging · Busola · OpenClaw · PostgreSQL

Original idea, design and implementation by quovadis.

Co-written by Lulu and Ted, my AI assistants

 

Appendix: Configuration Snippets

The four configuration artefacts referenced throughout the post, consolidated for copy-paste. All <PLACEHOLDER> values are cluster-specific — substitute your own. Per-token pricing values are omitted from the snippets; see SAP Note 3437766 for the authoritative model catalog and current pricing (S-user login required).

A. LiteLLM config.yaml — SAP AI Core provider setup

 
model_list:
  # Anthropic via SAP AI Core (backed by AWS Bedrock upstream).
  # The double-dash separator is SAP's model-naming convention for grouped providers.
  - model_name: anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet   # backing model for Goose v2 diagnostic pipeline
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet
      max_tokens: 32000
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>   # see SAP Note 3437766
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  - model_name: anthropic--claude-4.7-opus     # used by OpenClaw interactive sessions
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.7-opus
      max_tokens: 64000
      extra_headers:
        anthropic-beta: "output-128k-2025-02-19"  # unlocks 128k output; verify it's honoured
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  # Azure OpenAI via SAP AI Core
  - model_name: gpt-5.5
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/gpt-5.5
    model_info:
      input_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_INPUT_COST>
      output_cost_per_token: <PER_TOKEN_OUTPUT_COST>

  # Tier aliases — callers reference these; backing model can rotate centrally
  - model_name: reasoning-fast                 # default for routine work
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet
      max_tokens: 64000

  - model_name: reasoning-hard                 # deep reasoning; ~9× cost of reasoning-fast
    litellm_params:
      model: sap/anthropic--claude-4.7-opus
      max_tokens: 64000
      extra_headers:
        anthropic-beta: "output-128k-2025-02-19"

litellm_settings:
  drop_params: true                            # mandatory — SAP AI Core rejects unknown params
  request_timeout: 600
  num_retries: 5
  retry_after: 10
  forward_client_headers_to_llm_api:
    - anthropic-version
  callbacks:
    - no_stream_on_tools.proxy_handler         # custom hook (see Appendix B)
    - prometheus                               # enables /metrics endpoint
  require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: false     # allows in-cluster metric-agent scraping
                                               # MUST be under litellm_settings, not general_settings

general_settings:
  master_key: os.environ/PROXY_API_KEY         # injected from K8s Secret
  disable_spend_logs: false
  database_url: "postgresql://postgres:***@postgres-postgresql.<NAMESPACE>.svc.cluster.local:5432/litellm"

Environment variables. AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_BASE_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET, AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP are injected at startup from individual Kubernetes Secret keys. The LiteLLM sap/ provider assembles the AI Core service key JSON automatically.

Note on the orch/ prefix. The orch/ model prefix used by Goose v1 does not live in this LiteLLM config — Goose v1's GOOSE_HOST points to the Node.js llmproxy (port 4000), which detects the orch/ prefix, converts the payload to SAP Orchestration V2 format, and calls SAP AI Core directly. See the "SAP Orchestration V2 and prompt caching" section for the routing diagram. sap/ traffic flows through LiteLLM as normal.

B. The no_stream_on_tools pre-call hook (full)

 
import re
from litellm.integrations.custom_logger import CustomLogger

# gpt-4o doesn't exhibit the malformed-streamed-tool-calls behaviour on SAP,
# and some upstream callers expect streaming to remain enabled for it.
STREAMING_EXEMPT_MODELS = {"gpt-4o"}

# Matches SAP Orchestration placeholder syntax like `{{?placeholder_name}}`
_SAP_TEMPLATE_RE = re.compile(r'\{\{\s*\?[^}]*\}\}')


def _normalize_content(content):
    """
    Fix two SAP AI Core inference-endpoint quirks:

    1. Strip `{{?...}}` Orchestration template placeholders — when these leak
       into the inference path they cause: 400 Unused parameters: ['placeholder']

    2. Flatten pure-text content lists to a plain string — the inference endpoint
       rejects [{"type":"text","text":"..."}] with: 400 Unused parameters: ['text']
       Mixed lists (vision blocks etc.) are left intact.
    """
    if isinstance(content, str):
        return _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', content)

    if isinstance(content, list):
        all_text = (
            len(content) > 0
            and all(
                isinstance(b, dict) and b.get("type") == "text"
                for b in content
            )
        )

        if all_text:
            return ' '.join(
                _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b.get('text', ''))
                for b in content
            )
        # Mixed content (e.g. vision) — strip placeholders from text blocks only
        return [
            {**b, 'text': _SAP_TEMPLATE_RE.sub('', b['text'])}
            if isinstance(b, dict) and b.get('type') == 'text' and 'text' in b
            else b
            for b in content
        ]

    return content


class NoStreamOnTools(CustomLogger):
    async def async_pre_call_hook(self, user_api_key_dict, cache, data, call_type):
        model = data.get("model", "") or ""
        tools = data.get("tools") or []

        # Disable streaming when tools are present — Claude Opus returns malformed
        # streamed tool-call responses through SAP's LLM Module
        if data.get("stream") and tools and model not in STREAMING_EXEMPT_MODELS:
            data["stream"] = False

        # Normalize all message content
        for msg in (data.get("messages") or []):
            if "content" in msg:
                msg["content"] = _normalize_content(msg["content"])

        return data


proxy_handler = NoStreamOnTools()

Mount this file into the LiteLLM container at /app/no_stream_on_tools.py and register it in litellm_settings.callbacks as shown in Appendix A.

C. Kyma Telemetry: pod annotations + MetricPipeline

LiteLLM Deployment pod-template annotations:

 
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port:   "4001"   # LiteLLM API + metrics port
        prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

Pushgateway Deployment pod-template annotations:

 
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port:   "9091"
        prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"

MetricPipeline CR:

 
apiVersion: telemetry.kyma-project.io/v1beta1
kind: MetricPipeline
metadata:
  name: sap-cloud-logging
  namespace: kyma-system
spec:
  input:
    prometheus:
      enabled: true
      namespaces:
        include:
          - <NAMESPACE_WITH_LITELLM>   # e.g. default — where LiteLLM + Pushgateway run
          - <NAMESPACE_WITH_KYMA>      # e.g. kube-system — for Cilium / Hubble metrics
  output:
    otlp:
      endpoint:
        valueFrom:
          secretKeyRef:
            name: sap-cloud-logging    # created by the SAP Cloud Logging ServiceBinding
            namespace: kyma-system
            key: ingest-otlp-endpoint
      headers:
        - name: cluster
          value: <SHOOT_NAME>          # per-cluster attribution in OpenSearch
      tls:
        cert:
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: sap-cloud-logging
              namespace: kyma-system
              key: ingest-otlp-cert
        key:
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: sap-cloud-logging
              namespace: kyma-system
              key: ingest-otlp-key

The sap-cloud-logging Secret is provisioned via a ServiceBinding to a ServiceInstance referencing the central SAP Cloud Logging standard-plan instance. <SHOOT_NAME> is the Gardener shoot name (or any stable cluster identifier) — it becomes the cluster label in OpenSearch, enabling per-cluster filters and the fleet-wide breakdown in the shipped dashboard.

D. OpenClaw model routing config

The relevant section of openclaw.json showing the tier-alias pattern:

{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-fast",
        "fallbacks": [
          "litellm/reasoning-hard",
          "litellm/gpt-5.5",
          "litellm/anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet",
          "litellm/gpt-4.1",
          "litellm/mistralai--mistral-large-instruct"
        ]
      },
      "pdfModel": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-hard",
        "fallbacks": ["litellm/reasoning-fast", "litellm/gpt-4.1"]
      },
      "imageModel": {
        "primary": "litellm/reasoning-hard",
