        "fallbacks": ["litellm/reasoning-fast", "litellm/gpt-4.1"]
      }
    }
  },
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "litellm": {
        "baseUrl": "http://litellm-proxy.<NAMESPACE>.svc.cluster.local:4001",
        "apiKey": {"source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LITELLM_API_KEY"},
        "api": "openai-completions",
        "models": [
          {
            "id": "anthropic--claude-4.7-opus",
            "name": "Claude 4.7 Opus via SAP AI Core",
            "maxTokens": 64000,
            "reasoning": true,
            "input": ["text", "image"],
            "cost": {"input": "<INPUT_COST>", "output": "<OUTPUT_COST>"}
          },
          {
            "id": "anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet",
            "name": "Claude 4.6 Sonnet via SAP AI Core",
            "maxTokens": 64000,
            "reasoning": true,
            "input": ["text", "image"],
            "cost": {"input": "<INPUT_COST>", "output": "<OUTPUT_COST>"}
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Routing summary. Interactive sessions default to reasoning-fast (Sonnet) for routine work. PDF and vision-heavy image tasks route to reasoning-hard (Opus) as the primary — the extra cost is worth it for accuracy on vision inputs. Callers only ever reference tier aliases, never underlying model IDs, so backing models can rotate centrally without touching agent configs.

Diagnostic pipeline exception. Goose v1 and v2 in the dev-toolbox RGD hard-pin orch/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet and sap/anthropic--claude-4.5-sonnet directly (not through the tier alias) so the diagnostic bill is stable and doesn't follow central tier rotation. This is deliberate.

References

 

ComponentLink
Goosehttps://block-goose.mintlify.app/ · https://github.com/aaif-goose/goose
LiteLLMhttps://docs.litellm.ai/
KRO (Kube Resource Orchestrator)https://kro.run/
Kyma projecthttps://kyma-project.io/
Kyma Telemetry MetricPipelinehttps://kyma-project.io/external-content/telemetry-manager/docs/user/resources/05-metricpipeline.html
Kyma Telemetry Manager (repo)https://github.com/kyma-project/telemetry-manager
SAP AI Corehttps://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core
SAP AI Core (Discovery Center)https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-ai-core?region=all
SAP Cloud Logginghttps://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-logging
SAP BTP Kyma Runtimehttps://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/kyma-environment
Busolahttps://github.com/kyma-project/busola
OpenClawhttps://openclaw.ai/ · https://docs.openclaw.ai/
Prometheus Pushgatewayhttps://github.com/prometheus/pushgateway
Anthropic prompt cachinghttps://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching
SAP Note 3437766 — SAP AI Core model catalog & pricing (S-user login required)https://me.sap.com/notes/3437766
Related — Running Claude Code on SAP Generative AI Hub via LiteLLM (SAP Community)https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/running-claude-code-on-sap-generative-ai-hub-sap-ai-core-via-litellm/ba-p/14409432

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 1
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave  #1 (April-May 2026)

1. Gainsight Customer Success Management for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

 

Join Emma Falldorf, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest integrations between Gainsight Customer Success Management and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. In this insightful video, we dive deep into the key benefits and usage scenarios of this powerful integration, provide a comprehensive look at how Gainsight enhances the customer experience.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:39 - Why did SAP invest in bringing Gainsight into SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
02:06 - Which concrete scenarios does Gainsight offer for SAP customers?
03:09 - How can customers adopt Gainsight in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
04:16 - Start of the Demo
04:32 - Demo: Sales-to-Success hand-over in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
06:54 - Demo: Customer Success Management in Gainsight
10:27 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 in Action

Join Crystal Zhang, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest Account Planning capabilities of SAP Sales Cloud Version 2.
In this SAP Product Inspiration Video, learn why intelligent account planning is essential for modern sales teams and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 brings structure, insight, and focus to key account management. Through a concise end-to-end demo, the video shows how sales teams can capture customer goals and insights, define strategies and sales plans, monitor pipeline health, collaborate through action plans, and generate AI-powered executive summaries—all within one integrated solution. Discover how Intelligent CX from SAP helps turn account insights into consistent, measurable sales success.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:56 - Why Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
02:19 - What scenarios does Account Planning support?
03:37 - How can customers adopt Account Planning?
04:26 - Start of the Demo
04:42 - Demo: Account Plan - Set Customer Goals, Utilize Notes and Surveys
05:56 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Business Figures
06:18 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Key Stakeholders
06:49 - Demo: Account Plan - Conduct SWAT Analysis
07:07 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Sales Plans and Targets
08:00 - Demo: Account Plan - Monitor Pipeline Coverage
08:13 - Demo: Account Plan - Specify Action Plans
08:43 - Demo: Account Plan - Track Progress in a Gantt Chart
09:00 - Demo: Account Plan - Generate AI-based Executive Summary
09:43 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

3. AI Scouting in SAP Sports One

Join Michael Kesselmann, Product Manager SAP Sports One, and Achim Ittner, VP for Sports at SAP, for an exciting overview on embedded AI scouting capabilities in SAP Sports One. In this video, you will learn how clubs and federations can leverage Generative AI to streamline scouting workflows, accelerate decision-making, and create high‑quality player summaries with ease.
Discover how SAP Joule enhances the scouting process by consolidating extensive written reports, answering performance-related questions instantly, and generating structured management summaries that support faster and more informed squad planning decisions.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
02:54 - Start of the Demo
03:38 - Demo: Scouting Reports in SAP Sports One
04:09 - Demo: AI-Assisted Scouting with Joule in SAP Sports One
05:05 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

4. Mondi's Business Transformative with SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, SAP Commerce Cloud and SAP Joule

Join Philipp Haspel (Strategic Advisor and CX Presales Expert at SAP) in an inspiring discussion with Richard Drosdek (Head of Customer Experience at Mondi) about the CX Transformation at Mondi. Watch this customer-led product inspiration video to learn why Mondi chose SAP Customer Experience solutions and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, SAP Commerce Cloud, and the SAP CX AI Toolkit are helping them modernize sales, service, and customer engagement.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:55 - Why did Mondi choose SAP Customer Experience?
02:04 - What specific CX solutions have been implemented?
03:39 - How satisfied are you and what value has been delivered?
04:44 - What are your future plans in terms of SAP CX solutions?
06:12 - Start of the Demo
07:33 - Demo: Account and Activity Management
09:28 - Demo: Account Synopsis
10:19 - Demo: Lead Booster
11:20 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

Back to main page.

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026 - Wave 2
entry.content:
Hi everyone, on behalf of the CX Product Success team at SAP, I'm delighted to share with you our latest deep dive videos as part of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026. 

Wave  #2 (June-July 2026)

1. Innovate and Grow your Business with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next evolution of digital commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to learn why SAP launched SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition and how it enables fast time‑to‑value for companies looking to quickly establish or expand their digital sales channels.
Discover the key differentiators of this integrated solution, including seamless end‑to‑end processes between commerce and SAP S/4HANA Cloud, reduced complexity, and a business‑user‑friendly experience. Learn how customers benefit from faster innovation, lower total cost of ownership, and scalable growth—while SAP manages the technical foundation.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:49 - Why did SAP release this new SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
01:38 - What are the key differentiators of this new solution?
02:39 - Which benefits do Commerce key users gain?
03:05 - What’s the business value for existing SAP customers?
05:12 - Start of the Demo
05:25 - Demo: Consumer-grade B2B experiences
06:37 - Demo: Activate your storefront from ERP data
07:48 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Connected E-Commerce across SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition

Join Daniela Stehle (Solution Value Advisor for Customer Experience at SAP) in an insightful discussion with Lucas Timm from SAP Product Management about the next step in connected enterprise commerce. Watch this product inspiration video to explore how SAP‑managed integration seamlessly connects SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition—eliminating complexity and enabling truly unified end‑to‑end business processes.
Learn why integration has been a long‑standing challenge in commerce landscapes and how SAP’s managed approach removes the need for custom middleware, allowing customers to focus on selling, order management, and delivering great experiences while SAP handles connectivity in the background.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Start
00:26 - Introduction
00:49 - Why is integration so important between E-Commerce and ERP?
01:34 - How is SAP-managed integration different from customer-managed integration?
02:31 - What does SAP-managed integration mean for SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition?
03:14 - What’s the business value of SAP-managed integration for SAP Commerce Cloud?
04:09 - Start of the Demo
04:24 - Demo: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public - Sales Order Management
04:36 - Demo: SAP for Me - Activation of SAP Commerce Cloud, cloud ERP edition
05:25 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Stores and configure Countries, Regions, Languages and Currencies
06:10 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Product Sales Catalogs, Content Catalogs and Digital Sales Channels
07:09 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Customers and Business Partners
07:25 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Price Visibility and Taxes
07:37 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Order Management and Fulfillment, Sourcing Strategies and Markets
08:07 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Setup Payment Options
08:21 - Demo: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public - Product Maintenance
10:12 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Product Enrichment using Business AI
12:21 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Portal - Catalog Management & Assortment
13:41 - Demo: SAP Commerce Cloud Storefront - Experience Shopping
14:14 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

3. SAP Engagement Cloud: Agentic AI Innovations and Google Cloud Partnership across the SAP Autonomous Suite

 

Join Nisha and Valentin from SAP for a strategic look at how SAP Engagement Cloud is redefining customer engagement in the era of Agentic AI and the SAP Autonomous Suite.
Discover how SAP Engagement Cloud extends beyond traditional marketing to transform enterprise-wide business signals into contextual, relevant, and timely customer interactions. Learn how SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Joule, and the expanded partnership with Google Cloud and Gemini Enterprise combine to create a powerful multi-agent framework that helps organizations deliver more intelligent and personalized engagement across marketing, commerce, sales, service, and operations.
In the inspiration demo, see how AI assistants and autonomous agents help marketers build data-driven campaigns, personalize content, optimize customer journeys, and make real-time decisions based on inventory, customer behavior, geographic data, and business signals. Learn how SAP Engagement Cloud enables organizations to move from isolated marketing activities to business-scale engagement, delivering relevance, agility, and competitive differentiation across the entire customer lifecycle.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:50 - How does SAP Engagement Cloud fit into the SAP Autonomous Enterprise strategy?
01:51 - How does SAP think about customer data and Agentic AI to elevate engagements?
04:32 - How can engagement become a competitive differentiator for a company?
05:55 - Demo: SAP Engagement Cloud and Google Gemini
07:27 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

 

Back to main page.

entry.title: Explore SAP's New Joule Early Adopter Care Programs: Answers to Your Most Asked Questions
entry.content:
If you followed the announcements at Sapphire 2026, you've probably noticed that Joule is evolving rapidly. Between Joule Work, Joule Studio, and the new desktop experience, many customers are asking the same question:

"Where do I start, and which of these programs is relevant for my organization?"

To help answer that question, SAP has launched several Early Adopter Care (EAC) programs that give selected customers early access to upcoming Joule innovations while working directly with SAP product teams.

In this guide, I'll break down the four currently available Joule EAC programs, explain the differences between them, clarify common areas of confusion, and help you determine which opportunity best aligns with your business and AI adoption goals:

  1. Program Overview & What's New
  2. Registration & Process
  3. Eligibility & Prerequisites
  4. Joule Work & Joule Studio
  5. Joule with Knowledge Graph
  6. SAP Business AI Agents
  7. Joule Work Desktop App
  8. Trust, Data & Security
  9. Support & Next Steps
  10. Continue Your Joule Learning Journey

 

1 — Program Overview & What's New

Q1 I already have Joule — what's actually new, and why should I join an EAC?

Classic Joule (the version most customers have today) is a scenario-based assistant: you trigger predefined Joule skills within individual SAP applications — for example, asking Joule a question inside S/4HANA or SuccessFactors. It works well within an app context but requires users to know where to go and what Joule can do there.

The new generation announced at Sapphire 2026 is a fundamental shift in three areas:

  • Joule Work (Engagement Layer): A single, full screen entry point that sits above all your SAP applications. Instead of going to an app and then asking Joule, you express your intent directly and Joule surfaces the right data, agents, and actions from across connected systems. It introduces Spaces: dynamic workspaces that aggregate cross-application context for a specific goal. See how Joule takes action across systems to achieve people’s goals.
  • Agent Thinking: A new execution runtime replacing the legacy orchestration engine. It enables true agentic behavior: multi-step planning, tool calling, human-in-the-loop approval, and secure sandboxed execution — far beyond classic question-and-answer interactions.
  • Joule Studio 2.0: A managed development environment to build your own enterprise agents and skills, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and deployable to SAP's infrastructure. Deep dive into the new Joule Studio and watch the demo.

The EAC programs are your opportunity to use these capabilities before GA, work directly with SAP's product teams, and influence the final feature set.

Q2 Is this 'Joule 2.0'? How does it relate to the Joule I already have?

There is no product called 'Joule 2.0.' It is the same Joule — the core AI assistant technology has not been replaced or versioned. What has changed is how Joule is provisioned and experienced:

  • Provisioning change — SAP has moved from customer-managed Joule (where the customer set up Joule components in their own BTP tenant) to SAP-managed provisioning and Joule and SAP-managed integrations to products (SAP provisions and operates everything). This is a change to how you receive Joule, not a new product.
  • Joule Work — This is a new standalone interface that bundles Joule's existing AI capabilities with new features like Spaces and the front-end for Joule Studio, creating a single point of entry for SAP's key AI functionality. It complements, rather than replaces, classic Joule.

If you hear the term 'Joule 2.0' internally or from customers, it most likely refers to Joule Work — the new unified interface. Correcting this terminology matters because it avoids the impression that existing Joule investments are being deprecated or replaced with a completely separate product.

Q3 What Joule EAC programs are currently available after Sapphire 2026?

SAP launched four separate EAC programs. Each covers a distinct Joule product area:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio — The new AI-native engagement layer (Conversations + Spaces) and the AI-first development environment for building agents. Registration ends July 15, 2026. (Campaign #3986)
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph — Joule grounded in SAP's Business Knowledge Graph for precise, context-aware answers — plus upcoming agentic reasoning and voice capabilities. Registration ends October 1, 2026. (Campaign #4219)
  • SAP Business AI Agents — A broad portfolio of autonomous AI agents across HCM, Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, and CX. Continuously open registration. (Campaign #3987)
  • Joule Work Desktop App — A standalone desktop app for enterprise knowledge workers with local file access, web search, MCP integrations, and custom skills. Registration ends July 12, 2026. (Campaign #4218)

You may register for one or more programs. Registering is an expression of interest only and does not guarantee participation.

Q4 The Joule Work EAC and the Business AI Agents EAC both mention agents — what's the difference?

This is a common point of confusion. Here is how to distinguish them:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (#3986) — Focuses on the experience layer and the development platform. You use Joule Work as your unified workspace (Conversations + Spaces), and with Joule Studio you build custom agents and skills for your own organization. The agents here are ones you create or configure yourself.
  • SAP Business AI Agents EAC (#3987) — Focuses on SAP-built, domain-specific agents that come ready to use out of the box — pre-built assistants for specific business functions like a Payroll Assistant, a Sourcing Assistant, or a Deal Closing Assistant. Joule Assistants are role-based orchestrators that translate user intent into action by coordinating Joule Agents — which are the underlying, task-specific AI workers that autonomously execute complex, multi-step business workflows using tools, data, and reasoning.

An important nuance: the SAP Business AI Agents (Autonomous Assistants) only require Joule Work if they are system-triggered — meaning the agent initiates an action automatically based on a system event, without a user starting the conversation. User-initiated interactions with autonomous assistants do not require Joule Work. If you are evaluating autonomous agents, clarify whether your target use case is system-triggered or conversational, as this determines whether Joule Work is a prerequisite. All agents — whether system-triggered or conversational — require SAP-managed Joule, as it provides the Agent Gateway that powers agent execution. SAP-managed Joule can be set up independently of any EAC program via SAP for Me, and can either replace an existing classic Joule deployment or run side-by-side with one.

In short: #3986 is about building and experiencing the platform; #3987 is about consuming SAP's catalog of ready-made agents. Many customers will want both eventually.

Q5 Can we join multiple EAC programs at the same time?

Yes — the four EAC programs are independent and stackable. You can register for and participate in more than one simultaneously, provided you meet the individual prerequisites for each. The most common combinations are:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) + Business AI Agents (#3987) — for customers who want the new UX layer and SAP's pre-built domain agents.
  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) + Knowledge Graph (#4219) — for customers who want the new UX layer with deep grounding in their SAP data.
  • Desktop App (#4218) as an add-on — it has the lightest prerequisites and can run independently of the other three programs.

Note that each program has its own onboarding, project team requirements, and feedback commitments. Make sure you have the internal capacity to engage meaningfully with each program you join.

 

2 — Registration & Process

Q6 Where and how do I sign up?

All four programs are hosted on the SAP Influence portal at influence.sap.com. Log in with your SAP Universal ID, search for the campaign by name or number, and click 'Register.' Registration deadlines:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio — Register by July 15, 2026 (Campaign #3986)
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph — Register by October 1, 2026 (Campaign #4219)
  • SAP Business AI Agents — Continuously open (Campaign #3987)
  • Joule Work Desktop App — Register by July 12, 2026 (Campaign #4218)

If you experience issues with the portal, SAP recommends using the Chrome browser. For support, contact your SAP Account Executive or the EAC Program Lead.

Q7 I submitted my registration — what happens next, and how long until I get access?

Submitting the registration form is an expression of interest only. The process after registration:

  • SAP reviews your application against program criteria and available capacity. This typically takes a few weeks.
  • If accepted, SAP contacts you (via your Account Executive or the EAC Program Lead) to confirm participation and discuss onboarding.
  • Onboarding includes verifying prerequisites (e.g., SAP-managed Joule migration if needed), signing the EAC agreement, and scheduling a kickoff with your dedicated EAC coach.
  • If not selected in the first round, SAP may contact you as capacity opens.

For time-sensitive programs (Joule Work & Joule Studio closes July 15; Desktop App closes July 12), register as soon as possible to maximize your chances of being considered in the first intake.

Q8 When will Joule Work reach General Availability (GA)? Can we plan around a specific date?

SAP has not published a final, committed GA date for Joule Work or the other EAC programs. GA timing is intentionally contingent on the outcome of the EAC programs — SAP uses the co-innovation phase to validate readiness before committing to GA.

As directional guidance: EAC programs are expected to run for 3–6 months before GA. Based on the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC targeting a June 2026 start and running 180 days, a Joule Work GA in the Q4 2026 timeframe is the current directional target. For other programs with longer EAC windows, GA is expected in 2027.

For planning purposes, do not commit to hard internal deadlines based on these dates. Use the EAC period itself to build readiness, develop use cases in Joule Studio, and train your team — so you are ready to scale at GA regardless of the exact date.

Q9 How long do the EAC programs last?

Program durations differ per campaign. The Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC runs for 180 days from the program start (targeting a July 2026 launch, ending approximately mid-November 2026). The Knowledge Graph and Business AI Agents programs have longer or rolling windows. Exact end dates per customer depend on when participation is confirmed.

The EAC roadmap progressively unlocks additional Joule Work capabilities:

  • August 2026: Discover feed, Jobs supervision, System-generated Spaces (autonomous assistants), Joule Action Bar / Omnipresence
  • November 2026: Shared Spaces (multi-user + agent collaboration), Scheduled Jobs, Voice (web), Compositional Analytics

 

3 — Eligibility & Prerequisites

Q10 Is EAC participation free? What will Joule Work cost after General Availability?

EAC participation itself is free — there is no direct charge for the early access period. However, you must hold the relevant Joule entitlements and have signed the terms & conditions (available via SAP for Me). Depending on the track, a Joule Studio license and/or Joule Work Spaces activation may be required.

Pricing for Joule Work beyond the EAC has not yet been finalized and will be communicated separately ahead of GA. EAC participation does not commit you to any specific commercial terms for GA. Raise commercial questions with your SAP Account Executive early if they are a gating factor.

Q11 We are on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or on-premise — can we join the EAC, and do we need to migrate first?

S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (PCE) customers can participate in EAC programs — and importantly, you do not necessarily need to fully migrate away from customer-managed Joule before joining. SAP's recommended approach for existing customer-managed Joule customers is a hybrid brownfield setup: you register for the EAC and new Joule components (such as Joule Work) are set up in an SAP-managed way, while your existing customer-managed components remain connected. You do not have to do a full cutover before the EAC starts.

Program-by-program eligibility for Private Cloud customers:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition may become eligible with future product enhancements. At EAC launch, S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is the primary supported system. Register and SAP will assess your eligibility.
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph (#4219) — S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is explicitly listed as an eligible Joule setup. This is the most accessible EAC for Private Cloud customers.
  • SAP Business AI Agents (#3987) — Private Cloud and Public Cloud are both in scope. Note that specific agents and assistants require a certain S/4HANA Private Cloud version. A compatibility list of agents by S/4HANA Private Cloud version is available for PCE Finance and Procurement (May 2026). Contact your SAP account team or visit our agent roadmap for agent-specific version requirements.
  • Joule Work Desktop App (#4218) — No S/4HANA dependency at all, and equally no S/4HANA connectivity — you cannot read from or write to any SAP backend system. The app works with local files and external services via MCP tools only. The most broadly accessible program.

On-premise customers: On-premise S/4HANA customers can connect their system to Joule — and by extension to Joule Work — provided they meet specific criteria: a RISE contract and a commitment to the 40% maintenance fee. If these conditions are met, on-premise is a viable path into the EAC programs. The Knowledge Graph EAC (#4219) and Desktop App EAC (#4218) are the most relevant entry points.

Q12 We are a SuccessFactors-only customer — which EAC programs can we join?

SuccessFactors-only customers have access to specific EAC programs:

  • Joule Work & Joule Studio (#3986) — Not available at EAC launch. Joule Work Spaces currently requires SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. SuccessFactors integration into Spaces is planned but not in the initial EAC scope.
  • Joule with Knowledge Graph (#4219) — Open to SuccessFactors customers. Joule with SAP SuccessFactors is an explicit prerequisite option. Recommended EAC for SF-only customers.
  • SAP Business AI Agents (#3987) — Open. The Autonomous HCM domain is designed primarily for SuccessFactors customers — Core HR, Payroll, Recruiting, Performance & Goals, Learning, and more.
  • Joule Work Desktop App (#4218) — Open. No SuccessFactors or S/4HANA dependency, and equally no connectivity — you cannot read from or write to any SAP backend system..

Summary for SF-only customers: register for Knowledge Graph (#4219) and/or Business AI Agents (#3987) as your primary paths.

Q13 Is 'SAP-managed Joule' a prerequisite — and what exactly does it mean?

SAP-managed Joule is a prerequisite for the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC and for the Business AI Agents EAC. It is SAP's new SaaS delivery model where SAP provisions, hosts, and operates the entire Joule infrastructure on your behalf — replacing the older customer-managed model where customers maintained Joule components in their own BTP tenant.

If you are currently on customer-managed Joule, an upgrade is required before full Joule Work participation. However, SAP's recommendation is a hybrid brownfield approach: new Joule Work components are set up SAP-managed, while your existing customer-managed setup remains connected during the transition. You do not need to complete a full migration before registering for the EAC — raise your current setup with SAP during the acceptance process.

Q14 Can our system integrator or implementation partner participate in the EAC with us?

Yes — the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (Campaign #3986) is explicitly designed for customers and partners. System integrators and implementation partners can participate alongside the customer, which is the recommended approach if a partner is involved in your rollout.

  • Your partner can be involved in onboarding, configuration, and Joule Studio agent development.
  • The customer organization remains the primary accountable party — the dedicated project team and point of contact must be from the customer side.
  • Partners should coordinate with the customer and the SAP Account Executive during the acceptance process.

For the other three programs, partner involvement is not explicitly mentioned but is reasonable in an implementation capacity. Confirm with your SAP EAC Program Lead during onboarding.

 

4 — Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC (Campaign #3986)

Q15 What is the Joule Work & Joule Studio EAC and what can I experience?

This is the flagship EAC for the new Joule Work experience. It covers two products:

  • Joule Work — A full screen entry point framework connecting users to capabilities across SAP through intent, context, and conversational AI. The EAC includes Conversations (conversational AI with read and write access) and Spaces (intent-based, generative workspaces aggregating data across your SAP landscape).
  • Joule Studio — A fully managed, AI-first development environment for building enterprise AI agents, apps, and workflows. Supports intent-based development, open tooling (VS Code, Cursor, Vercel, n8n, LangGraph, LlamaIndex), and deploys to a secure SAP-managed runtime with enterprise governance, persistent agent memory, and access to the SAP Knowledge Graph.

You can participate in both together (Joule Studio license + Joule Work Spaces activation required) or in Joule Work only (Spaces activation required). Combined participation is recommended for teams who want to build and consume custom agents.

Q16 What are the prerequisites and known limitations for this EAC?

Prerequisites:

  • Signed terms & conditions for Joule and the $0 SKU for Joule Work / Joule Studio
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as primary LoB system
  • SAP-managed Joule (hybrid brownfield setup possible — see Q13)
  • Joule Work Spaces activation (required for all participants)
  • Joule Studio License (required for the combined track only)
  • A defined use case and dedicated internal project team with assigned point of contact

Known limitations at EAC launch:

  • Joule Work Spaces supports SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition only
  • Only one S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system per tenant (no multi-system)
  • Custom agent integrations limited to SAP-managed and SAP-native services — no third-party system integrations
  • Document grounding not supported
  • Spaces are initially read-only (write actions via Spaces in a subsequent update)
  • Datacenter Regions: USA and EU only

 

5 — Joule with Knowledge Graph EAC (Campaign #4219)

Q17 What is the Joule with Knowledge Graph EAC and who should apply?

This EAC gives customers access to Joule grounded in the SAP Business Knowledge Graph — enabling specific, context-aware answers based on your actual system data rather than generic AI responses, covering the full range of SAP entities. The program also gives early access to upcoming innovations including agentic reasoning and voice capabilities.

Prerequisites — an existing Joule setup with one of: SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors, or SAP Ariba. Your Joule instance must be in the EU10 or US10 data center (more coming soon). Registration closes October 1, 2026.

This program is the recommended entry point for Private Cloud and SuccessFactors-only customers.

 

6 — SAP Business AI Agents EAC (Campaign #3987)

Q18 What is the SAP Business AI Agents EAC and which agents are available?

This is a broad, continuously running EAC covering SAP's portfolio of autonomous AI agents across five business domains:

  • Autonomous HCM — Career & Talent Development, Compensation, Core HR, HR Knowledge, HR Service, Learning, Onboarding, Payroll, People Intelligence, Performance & Goals, Recruiting, Skills, Time, Workforce Upskilling.
  • Autonomous Finance — Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, Billing, Cash & Treasury, Financial Consolidation, Expense Management, Financial Closing, Financial Planning, Governance, Tax & Compliance.
  • Autonomous Spend — Buying, Category Management, Procurement Contracts, Invoicing, Sourcing, Supplier Management, Travel.
  • Autonomous Supply Chain — Asset & Service, Business Network, Logistics, Manufacturing, Planning, Product Design.
  • Autonomous CX — Campaign, Case Management, Deal Closing, Order Management, Sales, Service Management, and more.

Prerequisite: an existing Joule setup hosted in the EU10 data center (additional data centers coming). Note that specific agents require a certain S/4HANA Private Cloud version — consult your SAP account team for version-specific compatibility. Detailed prerequisites per individual assistant will be published by SAP in the near future. Registration is continuously open.

Important: the Autonomous Assistants only require Joule Work if they are system-triggered (agent initiates action based on a system event). User-initiated conversations with these agents do not require Joule Work.

 

7 — Joule Work Desktop App EAC (Campaign #4218)

Q19 What is the Joule Work Desktop App — and how is it different from the browser-based Joule Work?

Joule Work Desktop is a standalone native desktop application for enterprise knowledge workers. It is a distinct product from the browser-based Joule Work and is explicitly not integrated with the Joule Work web/cloud version.

Key capabilities: 

  • Conversational AI interface (on-device)
  • Local file & workspace access within a secure sandbox
  • Artifact generation — documents, summaries, structured outputs
  • Terminal command execution and integrated web search
  • MCP Server integration — connects to SAP and non-SAP systems via Model Context Protocol
  • Custom / corporate skills for repetitive tasks

Prerequisites:

  • SAP for Me access including an S-User ID
  • Requires download and installation of a binary executable on the user's machine — MacOS and Windows supported
  • A defined use case and dedicated project team
  • No existing Joule setup required — broadest accessibility of all four programs

Key limitations:

  • No integration with Joule Work (web/cloud)
  • No out-of-the-box SAP backend system integrations
  • Not available under EU-Access restrictions — must not be used for EU-only work purposes

Registration closes July 12, 2026.

 

8 — Trust, Data & Security

Q20 Where does our data go during the EAC — and is it used to train SAP's AI models?

Data governance is a top concern for enterprise customers. SAP's commitments in this area:

  • No training on customer data — SAP does not use customer business data to train or improve its AI models without explicit customer consent. This is a core SAP Business AI principle and applies to all EAC programs.
  • Data residency — For cloud-based EAC programs, data is processed and stored in your designated SAP data center (EU10 or US10). The data center region of SAP-managed Joule can be chosen during initial set-up
  • Joule Work Desktop — local data — The Desktop App accesses local files within a secure sandbox. Files are not automatically uploaded to SAP cloud systems. Review the specific EAC terms for what telemetry, if any, is collected.
  • Agent actions & HITL — All agent actions with business impact in Joule Work require explicit human approval (Human-in-the-Loop) before execution. No agent writes to your SAP systems without your confirmation.
  • Verifiability — Every action taken by Joule Work can be verified in the backend via traditional SAP Fiori apps, and AI recommendations include reasoning so users can validate before acting. All agent actions are written to the SAP Audit Log, providing a full traceability trail for compliance and governance purposes.

For contractual data protection terms, refer to the SAP Data Processing Agreement and EAC-specific terms signed during onboarding.

 

9 — Support & Next Steps

Q21 What support does SAP provide during the EAC — and what happens when the program ends?

All EAC participants receive dedicated co-innovation support:

  • Monitoring of critical issues by an EAC Project Coach and/or the development organization
  • Direct feedback channels — surveys, feedback sessions, and community forums — feeding directly into the Joule product teams
  • EAC-specific release notes and product update communications
  • Onboarding support to configure prerequisites and set up your first Spaces, skills, or agent scenarios

After the EAC: participants transition automatically to the GA versions. Your configurations, Spaces, and skills carry over. Pricing for Joule Work beyond the EAC has not yet been finalized and will be communicated separately. EAC participation does not commit you to any specific commercial terms for GA.

Continue Your Joule Learning Journey

As the Joule portfolio continues to evolve, investing in learning today will help your organization accelerate adoption and maximize value when these innovations become generally available. Here are several recommended resources to deepen your understanding and help your teams prepare for adoption:

  • Understand SAP's AI Strategy: Review the latest Sapphire 2026 announcements related to Joule, autonomous agents, and business AI innovation.
  • Learn about Joule: Introducing Joule Learning Journey focused on Joule capabilities and business user adoption.
  • Stay engaged through our AI enablement formats:
    • Beyond Hype, AI live session format, which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI
    • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills Monthly Blog Series spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

The latest Joule innovations are more than new features, they reflect SAP’s vision for an AI-powered, agent-driven enterprise.

Whether you're exploring Joule Work or Joule Studio, the Early Adopter Care programs offer a unique opportunity to gain early hands-on experience and collaborate directly with SAP product teams.

If you're considering participation, start with a clear business use case, review the prerequisites, and engage your SAP account team early. The most successful EAC participants are those who actively co-innovate and provide feedback that helps shape the future of these solutions.

All EAC programs are expressions of interest only and do not create binding commitments by SAP. Functionality, dates, and prerequisites are subject to change. For the latest information, visit influence.sap.com or contact your SAP Account Executive. This document reflects the program scope as of June 2026.

entry.title: Creating an MCP Server in SAP Integration Suite (Trial) – A Hands-on Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Integration Suite now provides an option to create and expose an MCP Server from within the Integration Suite environment. I recently tested this feature in an SAP BTP trial account and wanted to share a short, practical walkthrough based on my hands-on experience.
The goal of this blog is simple: show how to create, publish, and test an MCP Server using SAP Integration Suite in a trial setup.

More details about available Integration Suite service plans and capabilities can be found in SAP Note 2903776 - SAP Integration Suite – Service Plans and Upgrade Paths

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • An active SAP BTP trial account
  • Access to SAP Integration Suite 
  • Required capabilities activated, especially:
    • Integration Cell
  • A test API or service that you want to expose
  • Basic understanding of APIs and SAP BTP cockpit navigation

Step 1: Enable the Required Capability

  1. Inside Integration Suite, go to Settings → Runtimes
  2. Check if the Integration Cell runtime is available (if not, please wait until your tenant has it)
  3. Enable the feature ~ takes a while but works (if not already active)

Step 2: Create MCP Server Configuration

Go to an existing or new Integration Package.
Create a new artifact of type: MCP Server

Then maintain the basic configuration:

  • Select the source type
    • In this example: API
  • Provide the required details
  • Ensure that the MCP path is unique
  • Select the required tools based on the API resources
  • Save the artifact

After the artifact is created:

  • Open the MCP configuration
  • Add the required resources
  • Save it as a version and Deploy the MCP Server
  • Select the runtime and virtual host
Once deployment is successful, the MCP Server is active.
 
 

 

Step 3: Publish MCP Server in Developer Hub

After deployment, the MCP Server needs to be published so that it can be discovered and consumed.

Go to Developer Hub and add new content.
Then:

  • Select the relevant business system
  • Choose the Integration Suite business system
  • Go to MCP Servers
  • Select the MCP Server you want to publish
  • Create a product
  • Publish the product
  • Subscribe to the product to get the required credentials

This step is important because the client tool needs connection details and credentials to access the MCP Server.


Step 4: Testing the MCP Server

For testing, I used Cline as the AI tool.
The high-level steps were:

  • Add the newly created MCP Server to the AI tool
  • Maintain the server URL and credentials (basic auth - enabled in base API)
  • Make sure the server is active and allowed
  • Ask a question that should trigger the tool call
  • Validate the response from the MCP Server

In my test, the AI tool was able to call the MCP Server and receive a response successfully.

Personal Experience

Overall, the setup was quite straightforward once the required runtime was available.

What worked well

  • MCP Server artifact creation is integrated into the familiar Integration Suite package model.
  • Publishing through Developer Hub makes the server easier to consume.
  • Tool selection based on API resources is simple to understand.
  • Testing from an AI tool like Cline gives quick validation.

Key observations

  • The MCP path must be unique, so naming needs attention.
  • Credentials and server configuration in the AI client must be maintained carefully.
  • For first testing, it is better to start with a simple API with basic auth enabled.

Conclusion

The MCP Server feature in SAP Integration Suite is a useful step toward exposing enterprise APIs for AI-based consumption in a controlled way.

From my hands-on trial account experience, the setup flow is understandable and can be tested end-to-end with a simple API and an MCP-compatible client.

For anyone exploring AI integration scenarios with SAP Integration Suite, this is a good feature to try in a trial environment first

References

  • 2903776 - SAP Integration Suite – Service Plans and Upgrade Paths
  • API-Centric Integration | SAP Help Portal
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) | SAP Help Portal
  • Create an MCP Server from an API Artifact | SAP Help Portal
  • Limits in API-Centric Integration | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent
entry.content:
The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent

As of 2026-07-10. Config snippets and CLI versions reflect the state of OpenClaw and the bx CLI at time of writing. TOOLS.md excerpts contain internal timestamps (2026-07-07/08) reflecting when that config was written — not the post date.

A small OpenClaw story about a real gap in the default tool lineup, and the surprisingly durable workaround: drop a binary on persistent storage, prepend $PATH, and let the agent shell out.

TL;DR

The problem:

  • My LLM agent framework of choice (OpenClaw) ships 13 web-search provider integrations.
  • Twelve of them need an API key.
  • The one that doesn't — DuckDuckGo — is currently bot-detection-blocked for programmatic use, so web_search returns empty results or 403s in practice.
  • And routing Brave / Tavily / Exa / etc. through the LiteLLM proxy in front of SAP AI Core isn't the answer either — search APIs authenticate per-caller, not per-model-request. That's a structural limitation of every LLM proxy, not an OpenClaw quirk.

The solution:

  • Install the Brave Search CLI (bx) as a static binary into your agent's workspace, add that path to tools.exec.pathPrepend, and let the agent invoke it via exec.
  • Tell the agent about it in TOOLS.md.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds (the workspace is a persistent volume).
  • ✅Works with any model — the search key never touches your LLM proxy.
  • ✅One binary drop, one config line, one TOOLS.md note. ~20 minutes end-to-end.
  • ✅Brave's free tier includes $5/month in credits — light agent usage fits entirely inside that.

Note: If you run an OpenClaw agent behind LiteLLM (or any other model proxy) and don't want to plumb search-API keys into your model provider, this is the pattern.

The gap

Here's the tools block from my live agent config, verbatim:

tools:
  web:
    search:
      enabled: true
      provider: duckduckgo
      maxResults: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 30
      cacheTtlMinutes: 15
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

✅Eight lines. Two moving parts. That's the whole story of this post.

The tools.web.search block enables the built-in web_search tool the agent sees. Provider duckduckgo was chosen for one reason: it doesn't require an API key. Everything else in the search provider lineup does:

 

ProviderShips asNeeds key?
DuckDuckGoduckduckgo-searchNo (public HTML scrape)
Bravebrave-searchYes
TavilytavilyYes
Exaexa-searchYes
SearXNGsearxng-searchDepends on your instance
Perplexityperplexity-searchYes
Geminigemini-searchYes (Google AI)
Grokgrok-searchYes (xAI)
Kimikimi-searchYes
MiniMaxminimax-searchYes
Ollamaollama-searchLocal runtime
Parallelparallel-searchYes
FirecrawlfirecrawlYes

For a proof-of-concept in a fresh workspace, DuckDuckGo is the frictionless choice. In production, it's the only keyless option — and it turns out to be unreliable enough that I stopped using it.

Why not just add a search key to the LiteLLM config?

The obvious question if you already run LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core: can't I plumb a Brave/Tavily/Exa key through the same proxy that fronts my models?

Short answer: no.

  • LiteLLM (and every other model proxy — LangSmith proxy, LiteLLM Gateway, custom internal ones) is a model proxy.
  • It translates OpenAI-compatible chat/completions calls to whatever backend serves them.
  • It has no concept of "search tools" as first-class routable endpoints, because search APIs aren't a chat/completions verb — they're separate, per-caller-authenticated services.

You can, in theory, wrap a search API in a custom proxy plugin. But now you're maintaining a fork, and the proxy is doing something outside its job description. Not worth it for one CLI-shaped problem.

The other alternative is model-native web search — the search happens inside the model's tenancy, on the provider's side.

It works, but:

  • It only works for a subset of models (not the Claude tiers I route through SAP AI Core).
  • Results and cost are opaque.
  • You lose the ability to grep, cache, filter, or otherwise inspect raw search results.

I wanted a search path that:

  • Works with any model I route through LiteLLM.
  • Costs money against a search vendor I chose, on a bill I control.
  • Returns structured JSON I can pipe through jq and reason over.

That means: a CLI, invoked via exec, using a search-vendor API key held in a workspace env var.

web_search vs. web_fetch — don't confuse them

Two built-in tools that are easy to mix up:

 

ToolInputOutputWhen you'd use it
web_searchA query stringRanked list of results (titles, URLs, snippets) — no full page contentYou don't know which page has the answer; you want candidates.
web_fetchA specific URLReadable text extracted from that one pageYou already have the URL; you want its content.

web_fetch is not affected by the DuckDuckGo problem — it's a URL-to-content extractor and it works fine. But it can't find pages for you. If your agent needs to discover a URL first, it needs web_search — exactly the tool that's broken.

This is also why bx context is a nice primitive: it does both jobs in a single API call. Search + extract, one round-trip, one budget.

In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch (two round-trips), assuming web_search returned anything useful.

DuckDuckGo: why the default failed in practice

OpenClaw's duckduckgo-search provider is a lightweight HTML scrape of duckduckgo.com.

It works well right up until DuckDuckGo decides you look like a bot — which happens fast when an autonomous agent fires three queries a minute from a datacenter IP.

Symptoms:

  • Empty results: [] arrays for queries that clearly have results in a browser.
  • Occasional HTTP 403 with anti-abuse messaging in the body.
  • Intermittent "success" responses containing a CAPTCHA challenge as the first "result."

None of this is a bug in OpenClaw. It's the reality of scraping a search UI that doesn't want to be scraped.

The DuckDuckGo Instant Answer API is a real API but returns almost nothing useful for open-ended queries — it's designed for unit conversions and Wikipedia summaries.

⚠️ The worst kind of tool is one that looks like it works. Silent failure at the tool layer produces silent hallucination at the model layer — the agent confidently reports "no results found" when in fact the search never ran.

The workspace-binary pattern

A little context on where this agent runs:

OpenClaw is deployed on the same Kubernetes cluster as the diagnostic agent from the companion post. It runs as a long-lived sandbox (a pod) with a PersistentVolumeClaim mounted at /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/. Model traffic goes through the same LiteLLM proxy that fronts SAP AI Core — Claude Sonnet/Opus tiers, Nova, embeddings — all on the sap/* catalog.

Everything under ~/.openclaw/workspace/ is durable state. Everything outside it (system packages, /tmp, npm globals) is ephemeral.

Combine that with tools.exec.pathPrepend, which injects arbitrary directories at the front of the agent's shell PATH, and you get a durable tool-extension pattern:

  1. Drop a static binary into ~/.openclaw/workspace/bin/.
  2. Add that directory to tools.exec.pathPrepend.
  3. Tell the agent (in TOOLS.md) when to reach for it.

No skill packaging, no plugin manifest, no framework code change.

  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅No custom operator, no sidecar.
  • ✅Hot-reloads — no agent restart.

Here's the current state on my workspace:

$ ls -la /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/
-rwxrwxr-x 1 node node 2110240 Jul  7 18:15 bx

$ /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx --version
bx 1.5.0
 

2.1 MB, a single self-contained Rust binary from the Brave team. Sitting there since 2026-07-07, survived several pod restarts.

The one-time config edit:

tools:
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

That is the entire mechanical change. Hot-reloads, no restart required.

This pattern isn't Brave-specific. Anything that ships as a static binary, needs an API key you don't want in your LLM proxy, and returns useful text/JSON on stdout is a candidate: yt-dlp, ripgrep, a custom internal CLI, whatever.

Installing bx durably

⚠️ Install into the persistent workspace, not the container's global path. Otherwise the binary vanishes on the next pod restart.

Option A — one-off manual install (from inside the agent sandbox or via exec😞

mkdir -p /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
cd /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin

BX_VERSION=1.5.0
curl -sSL \
  "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v${BX_VERSION}/bx-linux-x86_64" \
  -o bx
chmod +x bx

# Configure the API key once:
./bx config set-key "$BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY"

# Sanity check:
./bx --version
./bx web "kubernetes ingress rewrite" | head -20
 

Option B — Kubernetes initContainer (production-grade, automatic on pod start, arch-aware):

- name: bx-install
  image: curlimages/curl:latest
  command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - |
      mkdir -p /workspace/bin
      if [ ! -f /workspace/bin/bx ]; then
        ARCH=$(uname -m)
        [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] && BX_ARCH="linux-arm64" || BX_ARCH="linux-amd64"
        curl -sL \
          "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v1.5.0/bx-1.5.0-$BX_ARCH" \
          -o /workspace/bin/bx \
          && chmod 0755 /workspace/bin/bx \
          && echo "bx installed ($ARCH)" \
          || echo "bx install failed"
      else
        echo "bx already present: $(/workspace/bin/bx --version 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)"
      fi
  volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /workspace
      name: workspaces-pvc
 

Option B is what we use in production. The if [ ! -f ... ] check makes it idempotent — on warm restarts it logs the version and exits immediately. BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY is injected from a Kubernetes Secret via envFrom.

💡 bx "query" (bare invocation) is an alias for bx context — both call the RAG-optimised extraction endpoint. The distinction matters when you're writing TOOLS.md, because the subcommand name signals intent to the agent.

The honest part: the agent doesn't discover this on its own

The model doesn't spontaneously pick bx over web_search. From the agent's perspective, web_search is a first-class, well-documented tool in the system prompt. bx is just an executable on $PATH, indistinguishable from curl or awk. The agent has no way to know that web_search returns garbage unless you tell it.

In OpenClaw, you tell it via TOOLS.md — a workspace-local file the agent is instructed to read for environment-specific tool notes. Here's the relevant section from mine:

## Web Search

**Preferred: `bx` (Brave Search CLI)** — the configured `web_search`
tool routes through DuckDuckGo which is currently bot-detection-blocked,
so use the `bx` CLI directly instead.

- Binary: `/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx`
  (also on PATH via `tools.exec.pathPrepend`)
- API key: auto-loads from `BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY` env var
- Docs: https://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/

**Which subcommand to pick:**
- `bx "query"` or `bx context "query"` → **default for RAG grounding**,
  pre-extracted clean text, token-budgeted. Best when reasoning over content.
- `bx answers "query"` → AI-synthesized answer with citations.
  ⚠️ **Not on current plan** — returns 400 `OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN`.
  Don't call.
- `bx web "query"` → raw web results (titles/URLs/descriptions).
  Best for finding sources or scanning multiple.
- `bx news "query" --freshness pd|pw|pm|py` → news with time filter.
- `bx images`, `bx videos`, `bx places` → specialised.

**Example (raw web + jq):**
    bx web "world cup 2026 quarter finals" | \
      jq -r '.web.results[] | "\(.title)\n  \(.url)\n"'
 

Two things worth calling out:

It documents a plan-specific gotcha. bx answers returns OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN on my current Brave tier. Without the note, the agent would call it, see the 400, and either loop or fall back to something worse. With the note, the agent skips it entirely. This is exactly the kind of environment-specific state that belongs in TOOLS.md, not in a shared skill.

It gives the agent a decision matrix. bx context and bx web return different shapes — context is pre-extracted clean text (perfect for feeding to a reasoning model), web is raw ranked results (perfect for scanning URLs). Documenting both means the agent doesn't have to guess. Writing just "Preferred: bx" would have it pick one subcommand and stick with it forever.

📝Your agent framework probably has an equivalent to TOOLS.md — whatever it's called (AGENTS.md, .claude.md, a system-prompt fragment, a project-specific tool doc), use it. This is where operational reality lives.

What bx context looks like in practice

$ bx "kubernetes ingress path rewrite rules" | jq '.grounding.generic[0]'
{
  "url": "https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/",
  "title": "Ingress | Kubernetes",
  "snippets": [
    "An Ingress may be configured to give Services externally-reachable URLs...",
    "The rewrite-target annotation lets you rewrite the path before forwarding..."
  ]
}
 

Three things worth noting:

  • Pre-extracted clean text. No HTML tags, no navigation cruft, no cookie banners. The snippets[] array is ready to inject into a prompt.
  • Structured JSON on stdout. Easy to filter, cache, and reason over programmatically.
  • Search + extract in one call. In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch — two round-trips. bx context collapses that to one.

Why this matters — how I extended Lulu's tool surface when built-in tools fall short

Both the Goose in-cluster diagnostic pipeline (from the companion post) and Lulu — my OpenClaw personal-assistant agent — share the same three foundations:

  • A Kubernetes runtime. A pod with a PersistentVolumeClaim, restarted freely, state preserved.
  • LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core as the model gateway. All model traffic — Claude tiers, Nova, embeddings — routes through it.
  • A clean separation between what belongs in the LLM proxy and what doesn't. Model requests: through LiteLLM. Everything else: local to the agent's workspace.

The LLM proxy is a model router, not a general-purpose tool router. Trying to squeeze search APIs into it inverts the abstraction. The right home for those is the workspace PVC, invoked via exec, with keys held in workspace-local env vars.

Practical implications for anyone running OpenClaw (or a similar agent) on Kubernetes with SAP AI Core underneath:

  • ✅The LLM proxy is not the place for search. Don't try to plumb Brave/Tavily/Exa API keys through LiteLLM.
  • ✅The workspace PVC is the place for out-of-band tools. Anything that needs an API key that isn't a model provider belongs as a workspace-scoped CLI, invoked via exec, with the key held locally.
  • ✅ TOOLS.md is the place to teach the agent about them. Skills are for shared patterns; TOOLS.md is for your environment and your PVC.
  • ✅The Kubernetes primitives you already have are enough. A PVC, a static binary, and one tools.exec.pathPrepend line.

Takeaways

  1. OpenClaw's built-in web search relies on DuckDuckGo by default. Check what provider is configured in your tools.web.search block — it may be silently returning nothing useful.
  2. You can't route search-vendor API keys through an LLM proxy. Structural limitation. Don't fight it.
  3. On Kubernetes, the workspace PVC + tools.exec.pathPrepend is a durable tool-extension pattern. Any static binary works. bx is one example.
  4. The agent doesn't discover local tools on its own. TOOLS.md is how you tell it.
  5. Document plan-specific gotchas explicitly. The OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN note saves the agent one failed round-trip every time it considers bx answers.
  6. Same runtime, different agent. The Goose diagnostic pipeline and the OpenClaw personal-assistant agent share the same cluster, the same LiteLLM/SAP AI Core plumbing, and the same PVC-as-scratch-disk pattern. That's a platform doing its job.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅ Works with any model routed through LiteLLM.
  • ✅ Fully auditable — the CLI runs in your workspace, on your bill.
  • ✅ Generalises to any tool that ships as a static binary.

References

 

WhatWhere
OpenClawhttps://openclaw.ai/ · https://docs.openclaw.ai/
Brave Search CLI (bx)https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli
Brave × OpenClaw guidehttps://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/
Brave Search API dashboardhttps://api-dashboard.search.brave.com/
OpenClaw config: tools.exec.pathPrependdocs.openclaw.ai → Gateway → Configuration Reference
OpenClaw config: tools.web.search.providerdocs.openclaw.ai → Tools → Web Search
SAP AI Corehttps://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core
LiteLLMhttps://docs.litellm.ai/
Companion post: Kyma Unplugged diagnosticshttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/autonomous-diagnostics-on-kyma-unplugged-goose-litellm-kro-sap-ai-core-in/ba-p/14435287

Stack referenced: OpenClaw · Brave Search API · bx CLI · Kyma Unplugged · LiteLLM · SAP AI Core · KRO

entry.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP): The Standard That Connects AI with Real-World Applications
entry.content:
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard developed by Anthropic that provides a universal way for AI models (like large language models) to connect with external data sources, tools, and services.

Think of it as a standardized "USB-C port" for AI applications, a common interface that allows AI systems to interact with the outside world in a consistent manner.

Key Goals of MCP:

  • Standardize how AI models access external context (files, databases, APIs, etc.)
  • Reduce fragmentation — instead of building custom integrations for every tool, developers use one protocol
  • Enable interoperability between different AI applications and data sources
  • Improve security and control over what data and capabilities an AI model can access

MCP Servers:
An MCP Server is a lightweight service that exposes specific capabilities or data sources to AI applications through the MCP standard. It acts as a bridge between the AI model and external systems.

What MCP Servers Provide:

Capability Description

ToolsFunctions the AI can invoke (e.g., run a query, send an email, execute code)
ResourcesData the AI can read (e.g., files, database records, API responses)
PromptsPre-defined prompt templates for specific tasks

 

How It Works (Architecture):

AI Application (Host/Client)  ⟷  MCP Server  ⟷  External System
   (e.g., Claude Desktop)          (bridge)       (e.g., GitHub, Slack, DB)
  • Host: The AI application (e.g., Claude Desktop, an IDE with AI)
  • Client: Maintains a 1:1 connection with an MCP server
  • Server: Exposes tools, resources, and prompts from an external system

 

Components of MCP:
There are 3 Basic Components of MCP

  1. Host
  2. Client
  3. Server

1.  Host

The AI application that the user interacts with directly.

What it does:

  • Manages the overall AI experience
  • Creates and manages MCP client instances
  • Controls permissions and security policies
  • Coordinates between the AI model and multiple clients

Examples:

Host Description

Claude 

VS Code Extension (Custom CAP MCP Server)

Serving MCP

ClineVS Code Extension
Joule

Via Joule Studio 

Add MCP Servers to Your Joule Agent

Custom AI App

Your own application

Responsibilities:

┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│            HOST                 │
│                                 │
│  • User interface               │
│  • AI model integration         │
│  • Security enforcement         │
│  • Client lifecycle management  │
│  • User consent handling        │
└─────────────────────────────────┘

2.  Client

The connector inside the host that communicates with a specific MCP server.

What it does:

  • Maintains a 1:1 connection with one MCP server
  • Handles protocol negotiation
  • Routes requests and responses between host and server

Key Characteristics:

Host
 ├── Client 1 ──── Server A (GitHub)
 ├── Client 2 ──── Server B (Slack)
 └── Client 3 ──── Server C (Database)
  • One client per server (never shared)
  • Handles capability negotiation at startup
  • Manages the session lifecycle

Lifecycle:

1. Initialize  → Client connects to server
2. Negotiate   → Exchange supported capabilities
3. Operate     → Send requests, receive responses
4. Terminate   → Clean shutdown

3. Server

The bridge that exposes external tools, data, and capabilities to the AI.

What it does:

  • Connects to external systems (APIs, databases, file systems)
  • Exposes capabilities in a standardized format
  • Executes requests and returns results
  • Enforces local access controls

Structure of a Server:

┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│            MCP SERVER              │
│                                    │
│  ┌───────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │  Tools    │  │  Resources   │   │
│  │           │  │              │   │
│  │• function1│  │• file://x    │   │
│  │• function2│  │• db://table  │   │
│  └───────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
│                                    │
│  ┌───────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │  Prompts  │  │  Config/Auth │   │
│  │           │  │              │   │
│  │• template1│  │• API keys    │   │
│  │• template2│  │• Permissions │   │
│  └───────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
│                                    │
│         ┌──────────────┐           │
│         │ External     │           │
│         │ System       │           │
│         │ (GitHub API) │           │
│         └──────────────┘           │
└────────────────────────────────────┘

 

 

What Problems MCP Servers Solve:

1. The N×M Integration Problem

Every AI application needed a custom integration for every tool or data source.

Claude ──── custom code ──── GitHub
Claude ──── custom code ──── Slack
Claude ──── custom code ──── PostgreSQL
GPT    ──── custom code ──── GitHub
GPT    ──── custom code ──── Slack
GPT    ──── custom code ──── PostgreSQL

Result: 2 models × 3 tools = 6 custom integrations (grows exponentially)

After MCP:

Standardized Protocol Layer

Instead of every AI app writing custom API code, MCP defines a common language for communication.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           AI Application (Host)                 │
│                                                 │
│   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐      │
│   │ Client 1 │  │ Client 2 │  │ Client 3 │      │
│   └────┬─────┘  └─────┬────┘  └──────┬───┘      │
└────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼──────────┘
         │              │              │
    (same protocol) (same protocol) (same protocol)
         │              │              │
   ┌─────┴─────┐ ┌──────┴────┐ ┌───────┴───┐
   │ GitHub    │ │ Slack     │ │ Database  │
   │ MCP Server│ │ MCP Server│ │ MCP Server│
   └───────────┘ └───────────┘ └───────────┘

How: Each MCP server implements the same protocol spec, it advertises its capabilities (tools, resources, prompts) in a standard format. Any MCP client can discover and use them without custom code.

 

2. Context Fragmentation

Problem: AI models are isolated from your real-world data. They can’t access your files, databases, or internal tools without complex workarounds.

Solution: MCP servers give AI models structured, real-time access to relevant context — your codebase, documents, databases, etc.

Discovery + Dynamic Resource Access

Step-by-step flow:

1. AI App starts → connects to MCP Server
2. Client sends: "What capabilities do you have?"
3. Server responds:
   {
     "tools": ["query_database", "insert_record"],
     "resources": ["table://users", "table://orders"],
     "prompts": ["generate_sql_report"]
   }
4. AI now KNOWS what's available
5. User asks: "How many orders this month?"
6. AI calls: query_database
7. Server executes query → returns real results
8. AI responds with grounded, accurate answer

The key: The AI doesn’t guess, it retrieves live data through the server.

 

3. Inconsistent Tool Interfaces

Problem: Every API has different authentication, data formats, error handling, and calling conventions. Developers waste time adapting each one.

Solution: MCP provides a uniform interface regardless of the underlying system, the AI interacts with all tools the same way.

Uniform Schema for Tool Definition

Every MCP server describes its tools using the same schema format:

{
  "name": "search_issues",
  "description": "Search GitHub issues by keyword",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "query": { "type": "string" },
      "repo": { "type": "string" }
    },
    "required": ["query", "repo"]
  }
}

Whether it’s GitHub, Slack, a database, or a custom internal tool the AI sees them all in the exact same format. It doesn’t need to know REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC underneath.

 

4. Security & Access Control

Problem: Giving AI unrestricted access to systems is risky. Ad-hoc integrations often lack proper permission boundaries.

Solution: MCP servers act as controlled gateways  you define exactly:

  • What tools the AI can invoke
  • What data it can read
  • What actions it can perform
  • What requires user approval

Permission Boundaries + User Consent

┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│         MCP Server (Gateway)          │
│                                       │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Allowed:                        │ │
│  │   ✅ Read files in /project/     │ │
│  │   ✅ Query SELECT on database    │ │
│  │                                  │ │
│  │  Blocked:                        │ │
│  │   ❌ Delete files                │ │
│  │   ❌ DROP tables                 │ │
│  │   ❌ Access /etc/passwd          │ │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                       │
│  Human-in-the-loop:                   │
│   ⚠️ "AI wants to send email. Allow?" │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘

How it works:

  • The server defines boundaries — only exposes safe operations
  • The server never gives the AI direct system access — it mediates every request
  • Sensitive actions can require user approval before execution

 

5. Lack of Composability

Problem: You can’t easily combine multiple tools in a single AI workflow (e.g., "read from my DB, then post results to Slack").

Solution: MCP allows multiple servers to run simultaneously, and the AI can orchestrate across them seamlessly in one interaction.

Multiple Servers, One Conversation

User: "Find bugs reported this week, check if there's a fix 
       in our codebase, and post a summary to Slack."

AI's execution:

Step 1 → [GitHub MCP Server]
          Call: search_issues("bug", since="this week")
          Result: 3 bugs found

Step 2 → [File System MCP Server]
          Call: search_files("fix for bug #421")
          Result: Found patch in /src/fix_421.py

Step 3 → [Slack MCP Server]
          Call: post_message(channel="engineering", text="...")
          Result: Message posted

How: The AI host connects to multiple MCP servers simultaneously and orchestrates calls across them in a single workflow. Each server is independent but composable.

 

6. Stale or Hallucinated Information

Problem: AI models rely on training data that can be outdated, leading to hallucinations about current state.

Solution: MCP servers provide live, grounded data — the AI queries real systems in real time rather than guessing from memory.

Grounded Retrieval Over Generation

WITHOUT MCP:
┌────────────┐
│ User asks  │ → AI generates from memory → Possibly wrong
└────────────┘

WITH MCP:
┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│ User asks  │ →  │ AI calls   │ →  │ MCP Server   │
│            │    │ tool/      │    │ returns REAL │
│            │    │ resource   │    │ data         │
└────────────┘    └────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                                           │
                                           ▼
                                    AI responds with
                                    verified facts

How: Instead of relying on potentially outdated training data, the AI queries the actual source of truth through MCP servers every time.

 

7. Vendor Lock-in

Problem: Building integrations for one specific AI platform means you’re locked into that ecosystem.

Solution: Since MCP is an open standard, the same MCP server works with any compliant AI client switch models without rebuilding integrations.

 

 

Example of MCP Server with Human-in-the-Loop (HITL):

An MCP setup where the AI can communicate freely (read data, gather info) but cannot act without explicit human approval.

Architecture

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         HOST                            │
│                    (AI Application)                     │
│                                                         │
│  ┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐       │
│  │  Client 1  │                    │  Client 2  │       │
│  └─────┬──────┘                    └─────┬──────┘       │
└────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┘
         │                                 │
         ▼                                 ▼
┌──────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────┐
│  SERVICE 1       │            │  SERVICE 2       │
│  Email Server    │            │  Database Server │
│                  │            │                  │
│  READ:    Free   │            │  READ:    Free   │
│  ACTION:    HITL │            │  ACTION:    HITL │
└──────────────────┘            └──────────────────┘

Key Principle:

  •  Read/Communicate → No approval needed
  •  Act/Modify → Requires human approval

Example: Email + Database MCP Servers

Scenario

A customer support AI that can:

  1. Read emails and database records freely
  2. Send emails or modify database records ONLY with human approval

Communication Between Two Services Through the AI:

The key insight is that the services don’t talk directly to each other. The AI Host orchestrates across them:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ORCHESTRATION PATTERN                         │
│                                                                  │
│  The AI is the BRAIN that coordinates across services:           │
│                                                                  │
│                    ┌──────────┐                                  │
│                    │ AI Brain │                                  │
│                    └──┬───┬───┘                                  │
│                       │   │                                      │
│          "read this"  │   │  "read that"                         │
│                       ▼   ▼                                      │
│              ┌────────┐   ┌────────┐                             │
│              │ Email  │   │Database│                             │
│              │ Server │   │ Server │                             │
│              └────────┘   └────────┘                             │
│                                                                  │
│  AI synthesizes information from BOTH, then proposes             │
│  actions that may touch EITHER or BOTH services.                 │
│                                                                  │
│  Example: "I read the customer's order history (DB) and          │
│  their complaint email (Email). Based on both, I recommend       │
│  issuing a refund (DB action) and sending a resolution           │
│  email (Email action)."                                          │
│                                                                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This cross-service reasoning is exactly where AI adds value a human would need to manually switch between systems, but the AI reads from both instantly.

The Permission Boundary Explained

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                  │
│                    THE PERMISSION WALL                           │
│                                                                  │
│   ────────────────── SAFE ZONE ──────────────────────            │
│                                                                  │
│   AI can freely:                                                 │
│   • Search customers                                             │
│   • Read order history                                           │
│   • Check ticket status                                          │
│   • Read inbox                                                   │
│   • Search emails                                                │
│                                                                  │
│   ═══════════════════ APPROVAL WALL ══════════════════           │
│                                                                  │
│   AI MUST get human permission to:                               │
│   • Send any email                                               │
│   • Delete any email                                             │
│   • Update customer records                                      │
│   • Issue refunds                                                │
│   • Close tickets                                                │
│                                                                  │
│   This wall exists because:                                      │
│   • These actions affect real people                             │
│   • They may be irreversible                                     │
│   • Errors have business/legal consequences                      │
│   • Regulatory compliance may require human accountability       │
│                                                                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The HITL Mechanism in Detail

When the AI wants to take an action, here’s what happens step by step:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                HITL LIFECYCLE (Single Action)                   │
│                                                                 │
│  ① AI decides an action is needed                              │
│     "I should issue a refund of $49.99 for order #4521"         │
│                                                                 │
│  ② AI calls the tool (MCP request sent to server)              │
│     → issue_refund({order_id: "4521", amount: 49.99})           │
│                                                                 │
│  ③ Server INTERCEPTS before executing                          │
│     → Detects this is a WRITE tool                              │
│     → Routes to approval gateway                                │
