│                                                                 │
│  ④ Approval request is presented to human                      │
│     → Shows: What action? What data? What risk?                 │
│     → Human sees full context before deciding                   │
│                                                                 │
│  ⑤ EXECUTION PAUSED — waiting for human                        │
│     → The entire AI workflow is blocked here                    │
│     → Nothing happens until human responds                      │
│                                                                 │
│  ⑥ Human responds                                              │
│     → APPROVE: Action executes, result returned to AI           │
│     → DENY: Action skipped, denial reason returned to AI        │
│     → MODIFY: Human adjusts parameters, then executes           │
│                                                                 │
│  ⑦ AI continues its workflow with the result                   │
│     → If approved: "Great, refund issued. Now let me email..."  │
│     → If denied: "The coordinator denied the refund because..." │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘


Risk Levels and Why They Matter

Not all actions carry the same risk. The system assigns risk levels to help the human prioritize their attention:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    RISK LEVEL FRAMEWORK                         │
│                                                                 │
│  🟢 LOW RISK                                                    │
│  ├── Close a support ticket                                     │
│  ├── Add an internal note                                       │
│  └── What it means for the human:                               │
│      "Quick glance, probably fine, approve fast"                │
│                                                                 │
│  🟡 MEDIUM RISK                                                 │
│  ├── Update customer record (address, phone)                    │
│  ├── Send informational email                                   │
│  └── What it means for the human:                               │
│      "Read the details, verify it looks correct"                │
│                                                                 │
│  🟠 HIGH RISK                                                   │
│  ├── Issue financial refund                                     │
│  ├── Delete data                                                │
│  └── What it means for the human:                               │
│      "Carefully review, check the amounts, verify policy"       │
│                                                                 │
│  🔴 CRITICAL RISK                                               │
│  ├── Bulk operations                                            │
│  ├── Actions affecting multiple customers                       │
│  └── What it means for the human:                               │
│      "Full stop. Review thoroughly. Maybe consult a supervisor."│
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Risk levels can also determine:

  • Which notification channel is used (Slack for low, SMS for critical)
  • Who gets notified (coordinator for low, manager for critical)
  • Timeout behavior (longer timeouts for higher risk)
  • Escalation rules (if no response in X minutes, notify someone else)


The Complete Customer Support Flow (Narrative)

Let’s walk through the example scenario as a story to understand how it feels in practice:

TIME: 2:15 PM

COORDINATOR: Opens AI assistant, types:
"Customer john@example.com says he was charged twice for 
order #4521. Look into it and fix it."

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TIME: 2:15:01 PM (1 second later)

AI silently queries Database Server:
→ query_customers("john@example.com") ✅ No approval needed
← Returns: Customer #789, John Smith, active since 2022

AI silently queries Database Server:
→ get_order_history("789") ✅ No approval needed  
← Returns: Order #4521, placed Jan 15, $49.99
   Payment log shows TWO charges of $49.99 on same day

The coordinator sees NOTHING yet. AI is still gathering info.

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TIME: 2:15:03 PM (3 seconds total)

AI has enough information. It synthesizes:
"Confirmed: Customer was double-charged. $49.99 × 2 = $99.98
 Only one order exists. Second charge is erroneous.
 Recommendation: Refund $49.99"

AI now needs to ACT. This is where the wall appears.

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TIME: 2:15:03 PM

AI calls: issue_refund({order: "4521", amount: 49.99})

🔴 APPROVAL REQUEST APPEARS:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ISSUE REFUND [HIGH RISK]                     │
│                                              │
│ Order: #4521                                 │
│ Amount: $49.99                               │
│ Reason: Duplicate charge                     │
│ Customer: John Smith (john@example.com)      │
│                                              │
│ [✅ Approve]  [✏️ Modify]  [❌ Deny]         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

COORDINATOR thinks: "Let me verify... yes, I can see the 
duplicate in the payment log. Amount is correct."

COORDINATOR clicks: ✅ Approve

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TIME: 2:15:15 PM (12 seconds after approval appeared)

Refund processes successfully.

AI now calls: send_email({to: "john@example.com", 
  subject: "Refund for Order #4521", 
  body: "Hi John, we've issued a refund of $49.99..."})

⚠️ ANOTHER APPROVAL REQUEST:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SEND EMAIL [MEDIUM RISK]                     │
│                                              │
│ To: john@example.com                         │
│ Subject: Refund for Order #4521              │
│ Body: "Hi John, we've issued a refund of     │
│  $49.99 for the duplicate charge on your     │
│  order. Please allow 3-5 business days..."   │
│                                              │
│ [✅ Approve]  [✏️ Modify]  [❌ Deny]         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

COORDINATOR thinks: "Looks good, professional tone, 
correct amount mentioned."

COORDINATOR clicks: ✅ Approve

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TIME: 2:15:22 PM (total elapsed: 22 seconds)

AI responds to coordinator:
"Done! I confirmed John Smith was double-charged $49.99 
for order #4521. I've:
✅ Issued a refund of $49.99
✅ Sent him a confirmation email

The refund should appear in 3-5 business days."

──────────────────────────────────────────────

TOTAL TIME: 22 seconds
WITHOUT AI: This would take 5-10 minutes of manual work
SAVINGS: ~95% time reduction while maintaining human oversight

entry.title: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

I'm Jayesh Sinha, an SAP Integration Suite Architect with over 11 years of experience designing and implementing integration solutions across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. Throughout my career, I have worked extensively with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), helping organizations build scalable, reliable, and maintainable integration flows. This hands-on experience with CPI's monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational challenges is what inspired me to explore how AI agents and the Model Context Protocol can simplify day-to-day integration work. This blog reflects my journey of combining enterprise integration expertise with modern AI tooling to make CPI operations more conversational and efficient.

SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CI, is widely used for designing, deploying, and monitoring integration flows between SAP and non-SAP systems. While CPI provides strong monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities, checking message logs, failed messages, runtime artifacts, and integration packages usually requires manual navigation inside the SAP Integration Suite UI.

In this blog, we will build a Python-based MCP server that connects to SAP CI and exposes CI operations as tools. Then, we will connect an AI agent to this MCP server so the agent can inspect CI logs, identify failed messages, summarize issues, and help with troubleshooting.

Instead of manually opening CPI, filtering logs, and reading technical payloads, the AI agent can call predefined CPI tools through the MCP server and return a clear summary.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI applications and agents to connect with external tools, systems, APIs, files, and data sources.

In simple terms, MCP allows an AI agent to use tools safely and consistently.

For example, instead of giving an AI model direct access to SAP CPI credentials and asking it to figure out API calls, we create an MCP server that exposes controlled tools

High-Level Architecture

User Queation -> AI Agent-> MCP Client -> Python MCP Server -> SAP CPI OData APIs -> CPI Logs, Artifacts, Packages, Metadata

The MCP server is responsible for:

Authenticating with SAP CPI
Calling CPI OData APIs
Handling JSON responses
Exposing CPI actions as MCP tools
Keeping credentials outside the AI prompt
Providing a controlled access layer

Benefits of This Approach

This solution provides several benefits.

It makes CPI troubleshooting more conversational. Developers can ask questions instead of manually navigating logs.

It separates AI reasoning from enterprise system access. The AI agent does not directly handle CPI credentials.

It creates a reusable integration layer. The same MCP server can be used by different MCP-compatible clients.

It improves maintainability. CPI API logic is centralized in one server.

It is extensible. More CPI tools can be added later as needed.

It supports safer automation. Read-only tools can be introduced first, while write operations can be added later with stronger controls.

Configuration

you can use CHATGPT/CLAUDE Desktop to connect to the MCP server.

1) Download MCP server in you local system from below GIT repository.

https://github.com/jacksnh/CPI-MCP-SERVER 

2) Modify "claude_desktop_config.json" to add the MCP server

"ci-mcp-server2": {
    "command": "python",
    "args": [
        "C:\\Users\\Username\\path\\to\\your\\server.py"
    ]
}

3) Restart Claude desktop and you will see below Server in Developer Section

4) Now your AI agent is ready to interact with MCP server

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Cloud Integration with an AI agent through an MCP server represents a meaningful step forward in how developers and integration teams interact with enterprise middleware. Rather than manually sifting through logs and navigating the SAP Integration Suite UI, teams can now ask plain-language questions and receive clear, actionable answers in seconds.
